title: Dedicated: per-tenant batching tier overrides + precedence rules
preview:
Context: As part of tiered batching defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient), Dedicated customers need an explicit override mechanism with deterministic precedence across (a) cluster-level config, (b) tenant overrides, (c) route/model policies, and (d) runtime defaults. We also need to make the effective tier/knob set explainable in telemetry (reason codes) and avoid surprises during staged rollout.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a Dedicated override surface that allows setting an explicit batching tier and/or explicit scheduler knobs for a tenant (optionally scoped to a route). 2) Implements precedence resolution in the policy engine + runtime config adapter so the effective profile is stable and debuggable: (highest) tenant override > route policy tier > tier default (model/hardware) > legacy batching fields (only when tier feature flag off) > compiled runtime defaults (lowest). 3) Enforces validation + safe-guardrails so overrides cannot violate basic invariants (e.g., max_wait_ms bounds for fast tier, max_batch_tokens upper bounds by hardware class). 4) Adds tests and examples, including regression coverage for SUP-48271 (override appears set but is ignored due to evaluation order).

Precedence details (new): - Dedicated tenant override: may set `tier` OR `knobs` (or both). When knobs are set they are treated as authoritative for scheduler parameters; tier is still used for observability labeling + latency guardrail selection. - Route policy tier: applies when no tenant override exists for that route. - Tier default: resolved using (model_family, hardware_class, streaming_flag, prompt_length_bucket) as inputs (workload-aware logic lands elsewhere; this PR only ensures the precedence chain is correct).

Backwards compatibility: - If tiered batching defaults feature flag is disabled, legacy batching settings continue to apply unchanged. - If enabled and the route config still uses legacy batching knobs, we keep honoring them for now but emit a warning log + a `profile_selection_reason=legacy_config` reason code to help migrations.

Observability: - Emits a new structured field `batching.effective_source` with values: `tenant_override`, `route_policy`, `tier_default`, `legacy_config`, `runtime_default`. - Adds trace attribute `batching.override_scope` (`tenant` vs `tenant+route`) when applicable.

Docs/Examples: includes a config snippet in `docs/internal/dedicated/` and updates the example Dedicated tenant YAML in `configs/examples/`.

Rollout note: This is a prerequisite for Dedicated opt-in rollout. It does not change Hosted behavior and is behind `feature.tiered_batching_defaults` and `feature.dedicated_tenant_overrides` flags.

Risk: Incorrect precedence can cause unexpected tail-latency regressions (fast tier unintentionally overridden) or cost blowups (cost-efficient tier applied broadly). Mitigation: exhaustive unit tests, golden config fixtures, and an integration test that exercises policy evaluation order across multiple tenants/routes.

Config example (Dedicated):
- Tenant override (global):
  tenant_overrides:
    - tenant_id: "tnt_3n6k..."
      batching:
        tier: fast
- Tenant override (scoped to a route):
  tenant_overrides:
    - tenant_id: "tnt_3n6k..."
      route: "chat/stream"
      batching:
        tier: fast
        knobs:
          max_wait_ms: 2
          max_batch_tokens: 2048

Test plan: - Unit: precedence resolver tests + schema validation tests. - Integration: config adapter produces expected effective profile and reason codes under mixed inputs. - Manual: apply override on a staging Dedicated cluster and confirm Console shows effective tier = fast and `effective_source=tenant_override` in traces.

Related: ENG-18426 reason codes PR (already in-flight) will consume the new `effective_source` field. ENG-18431 kill switch remains higher priority than any override (handled by runtime; not changed here).

title: Feature flag + staged rollout controls for tiered batching defaults
preview: Summary This PR adds feature flags, staged rollout configuration, and a kill switch for enabling tiered batching defaults safely. This is plumbing only; it does not change the scheduler heuristics or knob values (those land in separate PRs). Motivation Tiered batching defaults will be rolled out gradually (internal canary → hosted subset → Dedicated opt-in). We need: (1) a single global gate, (2) a staged rollout mechanism that can be tightened to an allowlist quickly, and (3) an emergency kill switch that reverts to legacy behavior immediately when p99/queueing regressions appear. This PR implements those control-plane/runtime toggles and the precedence rules. Scope / What changed Runtime feature flag + rollout policy Added a top-level gate TieredBatchingDefaults that controls whether the scheduler will attempt to select a tiered batching profile at all. When disabled, scheduler returns legacy batching config path (existing behavior). Added staged rollout controls: rollout_percent (0-100) and allowlists (regions, route IDs, and model families). Requests are bucketed deterministically by request_id hash when rollout_percent > 0; allowlists override percent (i.e., if a route/model/region is allowlisted, it is included even if percent is 0; if a route/model/region is blocklisted, it is excluded even if percent is 100). Kill switch Introduced kill_switch_enabled (runtime config) that forces legacy batching behavior regardless of all other flags. The kill switch is read from dynamic config and takes effect without process restart. This is intended for on-call use during canary incidents. Precedence / evaluation order Implemented explicit precedence: kill switch > blocklist > allowlist > percent rollout > default disabled. This is intentionally independent of any customer-level tier override precedence (handled in ENG-18430/PR-28503). Audit logging + safety rails All changes to rollout config are audited (operator identity + change summary) in control-plane logs. Config parsing validates percent bounds, mutually exclusive allowlist/blocklist entries, and rejects unknown region strings. Added guardrails to prevent accidental full enablement: if rollout_percent > 25 in production, we require an explicit acknowledge_full_rollout=true knob in the same change set (this is enforced by config validation, not the UI). Changed areas (high-level) runtime/config tiered batching rollout policy struct + parsing runtime/scheduler feature flag check + decision point span attribute control-plane config schema + validation + audit log entry ops admin endpoint: POST /admin/runtime/rollout/batching-defaults (internal) tests unit tests for hashing determinism, precedence rules, and kill switch behavior Files changed (representative) runtime/config/feature_flags.go runtime/config/tiered_batching_rollout.go runtime/scheduler/batching_profile_selector.go controlplane/config/schema/runtime_flags.yaml controlplane/admin/handlers/rollout_batched_defaults.go runtime/tests/tiered_batching_rollout_test.go Behavior details Deterministic bucketing uses Murmur3 hash(request_id) % 100 < rollout_percent. If request_id is missing, we fall back to trace_id; if both missing, the request is treated as excluded unless allowlisted. Reason codes: this PR adds internal reason enums used for logging/trace attributes only (SelectionPath=LegacyBecauseKillSwitch, LegacyBecauseFlagDisabled, TieredBecauseAllowlist, TieredBecausePercentRollout). The public-facing reason codes emitted as metrics land in PR-28473. Operational playbook notes (for SRE/release) Canary enablement sequence recommended by SRE: start with allowlist for internal routes + a single region; then add 1-5% percent rollout for the same region; only after perf canary + error budget checks pass expand to other regions. If fast-tier p99 regresses: flip kill_switch_enabled=true, then follow up by tightening blocklist/allowlist and reverting percent to 0. Testing Unit tests: precedence matrix (kill switch beats allowlist, blocklist beats percent, etc.), percent bucketing determinism, config validation failures. Integration test: control-plane pushes dynamic config updates and runtime applies within 30s (existing dynamic config propagation). Manual test: toggled flags on staging, verified scheduler path selection via tracing attribute runtime.batching.selection_path. CI Build: pass Lint: pass Unit tests: pass Runtime integration tests: pass Notes: had one flaky dynamic-config propagation test on first run; re-run green. Screenshots / Logs (snippet) Example audit log: actor=oncall@redwood action=update_runtime_rollout target=tiered_batching_defaults diff="rollout_percent: 0→2; allowlist_routes: []→[route_chat_streaming_usw2]" Follow-ups Not included: UI surfaces for rollout config (tracked in DES-771) Not included: rollout automation hooks used by release tooling (PR-28515) Not included: metrics/alerting for rollout config changes (ENG-18429) Reviewer notes / decisions Sofia: requested precedence docs + unit tests for missing request_id fallback (addressed). Sean: asked for explicit "acknowledge_full_rollout" safety latch >25% (added in schema validation). Priya/Jordan: requested config schema naming alignment with other runtime rollouts (renamed fields from tiered_batching_rollout_* to batching_defaults.tiered.*). Merge Squash merged to main. Deployed behind flags; defaults remain disabled in production until rollout plan executes.

title: Handshake escrow and client-abort semantics for streaming sessions (OpenAI compatibility)
preview: Motivation: intermittent client disconnects during long-running streaming responses cause two classes of problems: 1) clients that reconnect within a short window are unable to resume because the server immediately tears down session state; 2) client-initiated cancels (or network-triggered resets) have ambiguous semantics with respect to tool/function call idempotency and structured output flushing. This PR introduces a handshake escrow token and clarified client-abort semantics to make resume/reconnect and abort behavior deterministic and OpenAI-compatible. Key changes: - Introduce a short-lived session escrow ticket (Escrow-Token) retained server-side for a configurable grace window to support reconnect-and-resume for active stream sessions. The token is returned in initial stream response headers and via the OpenAI-compat protocol extension. - Define deterministic semantics for client-initiated aborts: a cancelling client sends a terminal CANCEL event; server replies with a 499-equivalent closure in the OpenAI adapter and marks the session state as aborted but keeps an abort-marker for the escrow window to allow idempotent retries for in-flight tool calls. - On reconnect-with-ticket, runtime replays the last stable checkpointed structured-output frame (if any) and resumes token emission; if the abort-marker is present the reconnect is rejected unless the reconnect explicitly requests idempotent-retry scope. - Add explicit flush semantics for structured outputs and tool-call boundaries: runtime now ensures a terminal JSON frame is emitted and persisted before freeing durable KV for that session. This reduces split-output and truncation cases seen in production. - Compatibility mapping: map client cancel to HTTP/499 in our OpenAI adapter and document the header-based Escrow-Token workflow for clients migrating from the legacy resume token. - Safety: by default escrow window is 30s, configurable per-tenant; escrow tickets are single-use and rotated on successful resume. - Tests and docs: unit tests for handshake lifecycle, integration streaming smoke tests for reconnect/resume, updated API docs and SDK examples (Python/TS) showing Escrow-Token usage and abort/retry patterns. The description below includes a short migration checklist and operational knobs.

title: Autorehearse promote controller with multi‑arm safety credits
preview: Motivation: Reduce risky promotes by exercising candidate variants against a rehearsal harness before any traffic cutover. This PR introduces an 'autorehearse' step in the promote controller that replays sampled requests against candidate model variants (dry-run) to estimate latency/correctness regressions and compute a multi-arm safety credit score. The safety credits act as a conservative rollback budget used by the promote planner to decide whether to advance, hold, or abort. Key features: 1) Rehearsal harness that can run lightweight replay using prefix-sampled traffic (configurable reservoir). 2) Per-arm safety credit calculation based on latency P95, error delta, and fidelity checks against golden prompts. 3) Integration with the rollout planner to gate phased promotes when credits fall below thresholds. 4) Rollback ledger and explainable advisor logs that record the reason for an automatic backout. 5) New telemetry metrics and an ops dashboard for per-promote rehearsal summaries. Design links: Linear ENG-8421 design doc and playbook, design sketch: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-8421/autorehearse-design. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed - [x] Rehearsal harness implemented - [x] Safety credit algorithm added - [x] Planner gates wired - [x] End-to-end unit and integration tests - [x] Docs and console UI snippets added - [x] Feature-flag gated and rollout-config opt-in Implementation notes: The controller will, when a promote with autorehearse=true is requested, snapshot a small traffic reservoir (default 500 samples) or use live sampling, replay the reservoir to candidate variants in parallel under a dry-run mode, collect telemetry, then score each arm into safety credits. Scoring gives weight to latency regressions (P95), model output drift heuristics (token-level edit distance against canonical outputs for golden prompts), and failure/error rates. Credits decay over time and are storable on the rollout object to persist across retries. Operationally the promote will only advance if aggregate credits for the target traffic fraction exceed the configured threshold. Rollback behaviour: if credits fall during a staged promote below the abort threshold, the controller will trigger an automated rollback with a detailed audit entry that includes sample diffs, latency histograms, and the deterministic reason code. Backwards compatibility: existing promotes without autorehearse remain unchanged. Feature rollout: gated behind rolloutConfig.autorehearse, disabled by default. Security and perf: Rehearsals run in dry-run namespace and respect quota and customer data controls; replay is sampled and rate-limited to minimize cost and avoid impact on production model caches. Related work: this PR builds on earlier rehearse and shadow frameworks and replaces ad-hoc manual dry runs with first-class controller wiring. Next steps: iterate on credit weighting, add per-customer KMS tagging for sensitive replay, and surface more explainability signals in the console.

title: adaptive batch scaling: seq-length-aware bucket resizing + padding-reduction heuristics
preview: Motivation: Mixed-sequence-length workloads (chat + long-form) suffer from padding waste and suboptimal GPU kernel selection when using fixed buckets. This change implements an adaptive bucket-resizing heuristic that observes recent sequence length distributions per route and dynamically resizes or merges buckets to minimize average padding while keeping latency SLOs. It also adds padding-aware scheduling that prefers coalescing requests whose effective token length (after prefix/KV reuse) aligns with available kernel tiling sizes. Key features: 1) Online bucket profiler: collects a lightweight histogram of observed input lengths per route over a sliding window and computes an optimal bucket partitioning using a cost function that trades padding tokens vs. queueing delay. 2) Adaptive resize policy: buckets can split, merge, or be retired based on utilization thresholds; changes are gradual and constrained to avoid oscillation. 3) Padding-aware scheduler: when forming a batch, the scheduler considers per-request predicted post-prefix length (prefix KV reuse is estimated from prefix hashes) and selects combos that minimize total padded tokens while meeting SLO latency budget. 4) Kernel hints: the batch builder emits a kernel_hint (e.g., 128/256/512) to the runtime so the kernel selector can prefer optimized kernels for that effective seqlen. 5) KV-cache integration: requests with prefix reuse get a reduced effective length for bucketing; we added safe heuristics for partial-kv reuse (best-effort reuse estimate instead of forcing cache-sharing mismatches). 6) Backoff safety: when the adaptive policy detects increasing queueing latency, it throttles bucket merges and reverts to stable config. Implementation summary: - runtime/batching/adaptive_buckets.{cc,h} : online profiler, bucket manager, policy parameters, unit tests - runtime/batching/padding_scheduler.{cc,h} : scheduler hook that ranks candidates by padding cost and SLO risk - runtime/kv/prefix_hash.cc : lightweight prefix hashing and reuse estimator + tests - runtime/kernel_selector.cc : accepts kernel_hint and scores kernels with seqlen-aware penalties - rtx-config.yaml : new tunables (bucket_window_ms, merge_threshold, split_threshold, kernel_hint_weight) - docs/perf/adaptive-batching.md : explanation, default knobs, rollout notes Tests and validation: - Unit tests for bucket splitting/merging invariants and prefix-hash edge cases. - Microbench harness: synthetic mixed workload (40% short-chat 32-64, 40% medium 256-384, 20% long 1024-1536) run on T4 and A100 clusters. - Canary on redwood/perf-canary for 48h with traffic shadowing. Results: - Median padding tokens per request reduced by 27% on the mixed benchmark. - End-to-end throughput improved by 12% on A100 and 8% on T4 for the mixed workload at target p50 latency. - p95 latency remained within 3% of baseline SLO; in a few extreme long-tail cases p95 slightly increased (~6%) before we enabled backoff; final merged policy keeps p95 within 4% after backoff tuning. - Estimated token cost reduction ~11–14% depending on workload mix. Rollout plan: Feature gated and off by default; enabled behind runtime flag --adaptive-batching=on and per-route opt-in via route config. Canary rollout steps included internal canary traffic then progressively more production routes over two weeks. Observability: Added metrics: adaptive_buckets.current_count, adaptive_buckets.padding_saved_tokens, scheduler.padding_cost_estimate, kernel_hint.effective_choice and tracing spans for bucket decisions. Migration and compatibility: No API or user-visible contract changes. Defaults are conservative; blob storage layout unchanged. Runtime knobs documented to allow ops to tune for specific workload mixes. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests - [x] Integration tests - [x] Canary run - [x] Docs updated - [x] Perf benchmarks attached - [x] Safety backoff added Known limitations and follow-ups: - Current prefix-hash reuse is an estimate; future work to support mandatory cache-sharing batches where exact KV alignment is required for more aggressive reuse. - We observed small oscillations in extremely spiky traffic; plan to add hysteresis and smarter smoothing. - Kernel_hint is advisory; runtime may ignore it in low-memory scenarios. Related: builds on prior seqlen-bucketing work (ENG-3401) and links to ENG-4189 for canary metrics instrumentation.

Key features: 1) Online bucket profiler: collects a lightweight histogram of observed input lengths per route over a sliding window and computes an optimal bucket partitioning using a cost function that trades padding tokens vs. queueing delay. 2) Adaptive resize policy: buckets can split, merge, or be retired based on utilization thresholds; changes are gradual and constrained to avoid oscillation. 3) Padding-aware scheduler: when forming a batch, the scheduler considers per-request predicted post-prefix length (prefix KV reuse is estimated from prefix hashes) and selects combos that minimize total padded tokens while meeting SLO latency budget. 4) Kernel hints: the batch builder emits a kernel_hint (e.g., 128/256/512) to the runtime so the kernel selector can prefer optimized kernels for that effective seqlen. 5) KV-cache integration: requests with prefix reuse get a reduced effective length for bucketing; we added safe heuristics for partial-kv reuse (best-effort reuse estimate instead of forcing cache-sharing mismatches). 6) Backoff safety: when the adaptive policy detects increasing queueing latency, it throttles bucket merges and reverts to stable config. Implementation summary: - runtime/batching/adaptive_buckets.{cc,h} : online profiler, bucket manager, policy parameters, unit tests - runtime/batching/padding_scheduler.{cc,h} : scheduler hook that ranks candidates by padding cost and SLO risk - runtime/kv/prefix_hash.cc : lightweight prefix hashing and reuse estimator + tests - runtime/kernel_selector.cc : accepts kernel_hint and scores kernels with seqlen-aware penalties - rtx-config.yaml : new tunables (bucket_window_ms, merge_threshold, split_threshold, kernel_hint_weight) - docs/perf/adaptive-batching.md : explanation, default knobs, rollout notes Tests and validation: - Unit tests for bucket splitting/merging invariants and prefix-hash edge cases. - Microbench harness: synthetic mixed workload (40% short-chat 32-64, 40% medium 256-384, 20% long 1024-1536) run on T4 and A100 clusters. - Canary on redwood/perf-canary for 48h with traffic shadowing. Results: - Median padding tokens per request reduced by 27% on the mixed benchmark. - End-to-end throughput improved by 12% on A100 and 8% on T4 for the mixed workload at target p50 latency. - p95 latency remained within 3% of baseline SLO; in a few extreme long-tail cases p95 slightly increased (~6%) before we enabled backoff; final merged policy keeps p95 within 4% after backoff tuning. - Estimated token cost reduction ~11–14% depending on workload mix. Rollout plan: Feature gated and off by default; enabled behind runtime flag --adaptive-batching=on and per-route opt-in via route config. Canary rollout steps included internal canary traffic then progressively more production routes over two weeks. Observability: Added metrics: adaptive_buckets.current_count, adaptive_buckets.padding_saved_tokens, scheduler.padding_cost_estimate, kernel_hint.effective_choice and tracing spans for bucket decisions. Migration and compatibility: No API or user-visible contract changes. Defaults are conservative; blob storage layout unchanged. Runtime knobs documented to allow ops to tune for specific workload mixes. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests - [x] Integration tests - [x] Canary run - [x] Docs updated - [x] Perf benchmarks attached - [x] Safety backoff added Known limitations and follow-ups: - Current prefix-hash reuse is an estimate; future work to support mandatory cache-sharing batches where exact KV alignment is required for more aggressive reuse. - We observed small oscillations in extremely spiky traffic; plan to add hysteresis and smarter smoothing. - Kernel_hint is advisory; runtime may ignore it in low-memory scenarios. Related: builds on prior seqlen-bucketing work (ENG-3401) and links to ENG-4189 for canary metrics instrumentation.

title: adaptive-signal-attenuator-and-stepwise-playbook-generator
preview: Motivation: On-call noise and low-signal alerts keep interrupting responders and dilute attention away from high-impact incidents. This PR introduces two complementary features: (1) an adaptive signal attenuator that temporarily mutes or down-prioritizes low-signal alert bursts based on multivariate heuristics (entity cardinality, recent noise history, error-rate delta), and (2) a stepwise playbook generator that converts canonical runbook templates into prioritized, step-by-step remediation checklists with embedded conditional telemetry checks. Both are exposed via the incident-bot core and a new CLI surface (oncallctl) and are designed for safe gradual rollout with canary attenuation and an explicit override flow.

title: add adaptive outlier suppression to canary evaluator
preview:
Motivation: Canary evaluators occasionally trigger rollbacks due to short-lived outliers (e.g., transient downstream hiccups or probe skew) that do not reflect sustained regressions. This PR introduces an adaptive outlier suppression layer that reduces sensitivity to isolated spikes while preserving detection of real regressions.

Summary of changes: Implemented a three-stage suppression strategy integrated into the existing canary scoring pipeline: 1) lightweight per-probe z-score normalization (robust to small sample sizes), 2) median-filtered spike-dampening that discounts single-sample deviations based on recent windowed variance, and 3) Bayesian weight recalibration that downweights probes with recurring discordant signals across slices. The suppression is parameterized and feature-flagged so operators can tune aggressiveness per-customer or per-route.

Why this is safe: The suppression is applied only at the evaluator decision layer (after metric aggregation) and does not alter raw metric ingestion or storage. The default configuration is conservative (minimal suppression) and must be enabled per-environment via rollout flags. We added comprehensive unit and integration tests and a canary job to validate behavior under synthetic noise.

Commits (high-level): - Initial implementation of suppression module and unit tests - Integrate suppression into canary evaluator pipeline and expose feature flag - Add integration test harness and synthetic noise scenarios - Dashboard panels and runbook update - Tuning iteration after reviewer feedback

Files changed (high-level): - pkg/evaluator/suppression.go: core suppression algorithms + docstrings and benchmarks - pkg/evaluator/pipeline.go: hook points to call suppression before scoring - pkg/evaluator/suppression_test.go: unit tests covering edge cases and small sample behavior - integrations/noise-harness/: synthetic traffic generator and canary job config - docs/runbooks/canary-outlier-suppression.md: operational notes and tuning guide - dashboards/canary/suppression-overview.json: Grafana panel definitions summary - go.mod / go.sum: updated deps for probabilistic utils - scripts/ci/integration_canary.sh: new CI job to run noise scenarios

Behavioral details: - Default window: 5m sliding window, min samples=30 for full Bayesian recalibration. - For windows with <30 samples the module uses robust z-score thresholds to avoid overfitting. - The median-filter dampening multiplies a per-probe anomaly weight by (1 - alpha) where alpha adapts to observed variance; alpha in [0.0,0.5] by default. - Weight recalibration applies a Beta-prior update to probe reliability scores; probes that consistently disagree with other probes have their weight reduced smoothly rather than clipped.

Compatibility and rollout: - Feature-flagged under env CANARY_SUPPRESSION_MODE with values {off, conservative, aggressive}. - Default for hosted perf-canary is conservative to minimize behavioral drift. - Observability added: per-canary suppression_ratio and per-probe adjusted_weight metrics exported with same cardinality as existing metrics to avoid downstream changes.

Testing and CI: - Unit tests cover small-sample and large-sample regimes, edge-cases where all probes disagree, and reliability decay scenarios. - Integration test runs the synthetic noise harness that simulates 10k requests with injected 2-10s latency spikes and verifies no increase in false-positive rollback rate compared to baseline. - CI: initial run failed due to flaky integration timing; added retries and deterministic seed in harness; subsequent CI run passed (see CI summary below).

Performance: Benchmark micro-optimizations added to suppression.go to keep evaluator p95 CPU overhead < 1% on our standard canary worker. Local benchmarks included in package bench tags; results attached to PR artifacts.

Runbook: docs/runbooks/canary-outlier-suppression.md includes instructions for toggling the flag, recommended tuning for bursty vs. steady traffic, and how to inspect adjusted_weight and suppression_ratio metrics when diagnosing missed regressions.

Review thread (selected): - Oliver Grant: \"Can we ensure this never masks a sustained 10% latency regression?\" Author: \"Yes — the Bayesian recalibration decays weights gradually; we also expose suppression_ratio across time. Conservative mode sets alpha max to 0.15 to keep sensitivity.\" - Priya Natarajan: \"How does this interact with multivariate canaries (latency + error rate)?\" Author: \"Suppression runs per metric slice; we keep cross-metric correlation signals intact and only adjust probe weights per metric. Added a test case where error-rate and latency diverge.\" - Ethan Liu: \"CI was flaky; add deterministic seed please.\" Author: \"Added deterministic seed for the harness and retries; re-ran CI and green.\" - QA comment: \"Add dashboard panels and add to SLO playbook.\" Author: \"Included dashboard JSON and updated runbook; requested product review for dashboards.\"

CI summary: - unit: pass - lint: pass - integration-noise-harness: failed (first run) -> fixed timing, deterministic seed -> passed on rerun - e2e (canary pipeline): pass with conservative mode enabled in test cluster

Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main. Merge commit: "suppress: adaptive outlier suppression for canary evaluator (squash)". Changelog: internal perf-canary: improved false-positive resilience under noisy traffic.

Follow-ups and TODOs: - Monitor suppression_ratio for first 2 weeks in canary clusters (ENG-4128). - Add telemetry-based autotuning prototype that will adjust alpha based on traffic entropy (separate PR). - Consider exposing per-tenant defaults in Dedicated offering after initial observational period.

Related work: contrasts with previous probabilistic smoothing PRs by focusing on probe-weight recalibration and per-window conservative defaults rather than global smoothing kernels.

If reviewers want a walk-through, I can demo the noise harness and dashboard panels in the next engineering sync.

title: Optimize INT8 dequant-fusion kernel and smoothquant scheduling to reduce p99 and token cost
preview:
Motivation: For the quantized inference stack we've been shipping two main bottlenecks: (1) the dequantization+matmul fusion path for INT8 kernels introduces extra memory traffic and kernel launches at each attention and FFN block; (2) SmoothQuant scaling interacts poorly with our scheduler and continuous-batching heuristics, causing variable p99 regressions under mixed-sequence workloads. This PR introduces three coordinated changes: 1) a fused dequantize+gemm kernel that accepts packed INT8 weights and produces FP16 accumulation with optional requantization hooks; 2) a scheduling tweak that biases smoothquant scaling application to the slower stage of the pipeline and batches compatible sequence shapes together more aggressively; 3) a lightweight KV-cache-aware fast path that skips per-step dequant for cached KV blocks and reuses FP16 cached keys when the model quantization profile allows it.
High-level summary of changes: - Implement fused_int8_dequant_gemm CUDA kernel with vectorized loads, int8->fp16 accumulation, and single-kernel scale+bias application; exposed via runtime/kernel_registry. - Add quant_profile metadata to model descriptors to select the optimal kernel variant at load time (INT8_PACKED, FP8_DYNAMIC, FP16_FALLBACK). - Scheduler: introduce SequenceShapeAffinity rule to group similar-length requests for continuous batching; add SmoothQuantWindow heuristic to only apply per-layer scaling on first N tokens of a request when streaming is enabled. - KV cache: store an optional FP16 shadow of int8-packed KV blocks for reuse; add LRU soft cap and transparent eviction. - Telemetry: new perf counters (kernel_time_fused_int8, kv_shadow_hit, smoothquant_applied) and unit tests.
Performance numbers (microbench on T4 and A10G; averaged across 50k requests of mixed lengths 16/128/512): - Baseline (old INT8 path): p50 12.8ms p95 38.2ms p99 78.5ms throughput 22 toks/s - This PR (fused + scheduling + kv-shadow): p50 11.4ms p95 29.6ms p99 49.3ms throughput 28 toks/s - Relative: p99 reduced ~37%, throughput +27%, token GPU time reduced ~18%.
Safety and compatibility: default model load remains unchanged; INT8 packed models must declare quant_profile or fallback to old path. This avoids accidental perf regressions for older deployments.
Checklist: - [x] Kernel implementation + unit tests - [x] Scheduler heuristics and e2e perf harness - [x] KV-shadow LRU + eviction tests - [x] Telemetry and console dashboard metric wiring - [x] Backwards-compatible model descriptor changes - [x] Integration smoke runs on Dedicated cluster
Implementation notes: commits include a sequence of focused patches (see commits field). The fused kernel uses 128-bit vector loads and tail-handling similar to other fused kernels in kernels/cuda/; we add a small inline assembly path for faster requant when supported by arch. The scheduler change is additive: SequenceShapeAffinity is a soft hint; it will not prevent immediate dispatch when latency SLO is tight. KV-shadow stores fp16 only when the quant_profile allows dequant-lossless shadowing.
Files changed (high-level): - runtime/kernel_registry.py: register fused_int8_dequant_gemm - kernels/cuda/fused_int8_dequant_gemm.cu: new kernel implementation and tests - runtime/scheduler/affinity.py: SequenceShapeAffinity and SmoothQuantWindow heuristics - runtime/kv_cache/kv_shadow.py: FP16 shadow store, eviction policy - model/quant_profiles.py: quant_profile schema and loaders - telemetry/metrics.py: new counters and tagging - tests/perf/test_quant_kernels.py: microbench harness and assertions - docs/quantization.md: updated guidance and model packaging notes
Commits (summary): - a1c3f8a: initial fused kernel prototype and unit tests - b4d2e10: vectorization, tail handling, and arch-specific paths - c7f9b01: expose kernel via kernel_registry and add quant_profile selector - d2a8f44: implement SequenceShapeAffinity scheduler rule and tests - e5b1c3f: KV-shadow store with LRU and eviction unit tests - f1039d7: telemetry counters and console dashboard wires - f8b6a22: perf tuning, CI fixes, and documentation
Update/Review conversation (selected): - Priya Natarajan (reviewer): "Great direction. My main concern is memory overhead for the FP16 KV shadow under high-concurrency. Can we bound memory and prioritize eviction for older sequences?" Author reply (Ethan): "Answered: KV-shadow uses an LRU soft cap with eviction triggered at 0.6*mem_limit and immediate reclamation to 0.8*mem_limit to avoid thrashing; added bench in tests to ensure p99 doesn't regress under pressure." - Liam O'Connor: "Kernel looks good; please add more comments around the arch-specific asm path and a fallback when not supported." Author: "Added comments and a runtime is_x86_avx512 check; fallback to scalar path included." - Sara Kim: "How does this interact with canary rollout? We need a safe toggle in runtime to opt-out quickly." Author: "Added runtime feature flag runtime.flags.quant_fused_enabled and a per-deployment override exposed to Dedicated/Private configs; tests added."
CI checks summary: - linux-build: pass - unit-tests: pass - perf-regression: pass (bench run) - flake8/lint: pass - integration-smoke(dedicated): pass
Merge outcome: merged via squash on 2026-03-05 by Ethan Zhao; merge commit f9c2d11. Post-merge actions: - Backfill quant_profile to community model registry (docs/quantization.md has migration steps) - Dashboard alert to watch kernel_time_fused_int8 p99 for next 72 hours - Coordinate with Customer Success for a small set of Dedicated customers to enable feature flag during next maintenance window.

title: fragmented-kernel-workspace-pool-kv-rolling-reserve-and-adaptive-batching-prioritizer
preview: Motivation: Under heavy continuous-batching workloads we observe fragmented temporary workspace allocations and conservative KV cache reservations that inflate peak GPU memory by ~15-25%. This PR introduces a kernel-fragment pool allocator, a compacting fragment coalescer, a KV rolling-reserve policy with age-based demotion, and an adaptive batching prioritizer that protects tail latency while increasing packing efficiency. Goals: 1) reduce peak transient workspace (scratch) footprint, 2) lower KV cache reserved overhead for workloads with skewed hotsets, 3) keep 95p/99p latency equal or improved under steady-state continuous batching. Changes (high level): - runtime/worker: implement FragmentPool allocator that suballocates kernel workspace into variable-length fragments mapped to kernel affinity classes; supports fast reuse and best-fit coalescing on free. - runtime/scheduler: add AdaptiveBatchPrioritizer which reorders queued requests to reduce internal fragmentation and avoid long-lived large-activation requests from blocking smaller quick requests (tail-latency guardrails). - runtime/kvcache: KV rolling-reserve: reserve window size per-model by observed hit-age histogram; demote rarely accessed entries from reserved to spillable on a time-decay basis. - runtime/alloc: optional lightweight compaction pass that coalesces adjacent free fragments during low-load periods. - tests & tooling: new microbench harnesses for fragmented allocation patterns, end-to-end perf scenario files, and flamegraph capture hooks for workspace churn. Implementation notes: - FragmentPool is lock-free per-GPU-stream for fast path allocation; fallback to pooled mutex for rare large allocations. - Coalescing is limited to a short-duration cooperative pass triggered by low utilization to avoid impacting latency. - Rolling-reserve tracks per-key last-access epoch and uses a small LRU-friendly structure to decide demotion; no synchronous CPU writeback in hot path. Performance summary (measured on 8xA100, config: steady continuous batching, mixed length): - Peak GPU memory usage: -18% median, -23% under the 95th percentile scenarios. - 95p latency: -12% (reduction) for short requests; unchanged for long requests. - Throughput (tokens/sec): +6% due to improved packing and reduced stalls. Safety and fallback: All features are disabled by default for existing customers; new flags are behind runtime flags and can be toggled per-deployment. Rolling-reserve defaults to conservative limits; fragmentation coalescer disabled on single-GPU hosts. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for FragmentPool api - [x] integration tests for scheduler reordering - [x] KV reserve regression tests - [x] perf harness runs (attached) - [x] docs entry for runtime flags Migration/compatibility: No API changes; runtime flags control behavior. Observability: added metrics: fragpool.alloc_count, fragpool.coalesce_time_ms, kv.reserve_demotions, batch.prioritizer_reorders. Related: ENG-5643 (workspaces), ENG-5711 (KV hotset sizing).

title: per-layer kernel occupancy probes + adaptive batch-window tracer (KV-stamp profiler)
preview:
Summary: Instrumentation and microbench additions to help diagnose layer-level kernel occupancy and batch-window interactions with KV-cache. This PR introduces a lightweight per-layer occupancy probe, a stamp-based KV-cache sampler ("kv-stamp profiler"), and an adaptive batch-window tracer that records contiguous request grouping behavior used by the continuous-batching scheduler. All probes are zero-cost when disabled and are guarded behind runtime flags and RBAC-controlled debug endpoints.

Motivation and context: We observed tail-latency regressions under mixed request shapes where a few large layers dominated GPU occupancy and caused scheduler-induced head-of-line blocking. Existing coarse counters were insufficient to map slowdowns to individual kernel launches or to correlate KV-cache reuse with batch-window size. This work aims to give engineers the tools to: (1) attribute occupancy regressions to specific model layers/kernels, (2) measure the effective batch-window (number of requests grouped per dispatch) over time, and (3) correlate KV-cache hit patterns with per-layer hot paths.

What changed (high level):
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.h/.cc: per-layer occupancy probe that hooks into kernel dispatch points and records occupancy (SM utilization estimate) + launch duration. Exports a compact proto-friendly record for tracing.
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.h/.cc: stamp-based KV-cache sampler that attaches a short-lived token to requests and samples cache hits/misses across layers to compute reuse windows.
- runtime/scheduler/batch_window_tracer.cc: adaptive tracer that logs when the scheduler decides to flush a batch, capturing batch composition, sequence lengths, and KV stamps.
- tools/microbench/occupancy_benchdriver: small microbench harness to replay recorded traces at configurable QPS and report distribution of occupancy and batch-window sizes.
- observability/dashboards/occupancy-dashboard.json: Grafana panel definitions and Query Language (PromQL/SQL) mappings to visualize per-layer occupancy heatmap and KV reuse curves.
- controlplane: added debug endpoint /debug/probes to enable/disable probes and stream sampler snapshots (RBAC-protected).

Commits (summary):
- 9f3d2a8: add occupancy probe skeleton and proto definitions
- 1b4a7c2: implement kernel hookpoints and SM-util estimators
- 6c8ef91: add KV-stamp sampling and tag propagation through request context
- d4a11b3: integrate batch-window tracer into continuous batching scheduler
- aebb2f0: microbench harness + runner + synthetic workload generator
- be3c442: dashboards and CI runbook instructions
- c7f0d9a: tests, docs, and RBAC debug endpoint

Files changed (select highlights):
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.h (new)
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.cc (new)
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.h (new)
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.cc (new)
- runtime/scheduler/batch_window_tracer.cc (modified)
- tools/microbench/occupancy_benchdriver/main.cc (new)
- observability/dashboards/occupancy-dashboard.json (new)
- controlplane/http/debug_handlers.cc (modified)
- docs/observability/occupancy.md (new)

Testing and results: Unit and integration tests were added for probe lifecycle and stamp propagation. Microbench harness reproduces a regression scenario: mixed traffic with occasional long sequences. Before instrumentation + adaptive tracer, under synthetic 500 qps workload with 5% long requests we observed 99th latency = 312 ms and visible spike in SM contention. After adapting scheduler thresholds (guided by the tracer insights) and enabling KV-stamp-aware coalescing, end-to-end 99th latency dropped to 245 ms (-21%), and average per-layer hotpath occupancy variance reduced by ~34% in the bench runs. Note: these numbers are from the microbench harness on a p4d instance; production effects would vary.

Backward compatibility and safety: All probes are opt-in via the /debug/probes endpoint and a runtime config flag (runtime.probes.enabled=false by default). The probe code avoids allocations on the hot path by using thread-local ring buffers and a lock-free aggregator. The control plane enforces that only users with the observability:admin role can toggle sampling.

Review conversation (selected excerpts):
- Jordan Lee (reviewer): "Nice work — this gives the visibility we needed. A couple of asks: (1) ensure SM-estimator doesn't allocate on every kernel path, (2) add a unit test for stamp propagation across async cuda streams."
- Maya Patel (author): "Addressed both: estimator uses a precomputed LUT and TLS, added stream-propagation test in runtime/probes/tests/kv_stamp_test.cc."
- Priya Natarajan (reviewer): "Grafana panels look good, but let's avoid adding expensive dashboard queries to the default ingest. Recommend materializing a short-term rollup table in the observability pipeline."
- Maya Patel: "Agreed — changed dashboard to use pre-aggregated metrics (probe_occupancy_bucket) and added guidance in docs/observability/occupancy.md."
- Luis Gomez (reviewer & approver): "Approving after small nits. Please document RBAC access in the ops runbook."
- Ops comment (internal): "Temporarily disable probes in canary until we've validated throughput impact at production load."

CI summary: Initial CI run reported flaky unit on runtime/scheduler tests (timing-dependent) which was fixed by introducing a deterministic clock in the test harness. Full CI (build, unit, integration, lint) all passed on final run. Perf check (microbench pipeline) executed on the perf-lab pool; results above included.

Follow-ups and TODOs:
- ENG-3922: Add continuous integration job to run microbench harness nightly against representative instance types.
- ENG-3938: Add end-to-end alerting that surfaces sudden increases in per-layer occupancy variance.
- Investigate tighter kernel-level hooks for future reduction of estimator error under model parallelism.

Merge: Squash-merged into main on 2026-03-05 after approvals. Change is feature-flagged and shipped behind RBAC and runtime flag to minimize risk. The dashboards and docs are included in the same PR to make rollout and troubleshooting immediate.

Related work: complements earlier occupancy counters and tracing exports but focuses on correlating occupancy with KV-cache reuse and continuous-batching behavior. Distinct from prior NVTX/time-trace changes by providing a low-overhead sampled profiler and an adaptive tracer targeted at scheduler decision points.

title: Real-time Runbook Compiler and Telemetry Stitcher
preview:
Context and motivation: On-call responders frequently have to assemble fragmented signals (alerts, traces, logs, SLO deltas) into an actionable runbook under time pressure. This PR introduces a Runbook Compiler service that composes a compact, prioritized runbook from a set of incident signals and a Telemetry Stitcher that pulls and correlates relevant telemetry slices across services. The goal is to reduce TTR and make playbook suggestions reproducible and auditable.

What this PR does (summary):
- Add runbook-compiler microservice (go) that accepts an incident payload and returns a prioritized runbook (steps + rationale + safety gates).
- Add telemetry-stitcher worker (python) that builds correlated telemetry slices (spans, top traces, key logs, metric cutouts) and applies automatic sanitization rules to strip PII/keys.
- Add ephemeral-debug-proxy (node) that can safely replay a limited subset of traffic against replica services in a sandboxed environment. The proxy enforces resource/time budgets and kill switches.
- Integrations with slo-toolkit sampler: sample sets are used to pick representative traces for evaluation and to detect regressions compared with baseline runs.
- Console + incident-bot UI hooks: new /runbook-compile command and a RunbookDraft view in incident context.

Design notes and safety:
- Sanitization: telemetry-stitcher runs a tag-based sanitizer followed by deterministic hashing for identifiers. We maintain a whitelist/blacklist config stored in vault. Changes to sanitization config require operator approval.
- Access control: runbook compilation is gated to oncall+incident-bot roles; ephemeral proxy ACLs are short-lived, minted per-session via the existing token broker.
- Resource constraints: debug proxy and stitcher are constrained by CPU/memory/QPS budgets and will fallback to a degraded mode that returns pointers to raw telemetry if budgets are exhausted.

Commits (high-level):
- 1a2f3d4 add runbook-compiler service + core compilation pipeline
- 2b3e4f5 implement telemetry-stitcher and sanitization rules
- 3c4d5e6 ephemeral-debug-proxy + sandbox harness with safe rollback hooks
- 4d5e6f7 ui: incident-bot /runbook-compile command and RunbookDraft renderer
- 5e6f7g8 tests: unit + integration + sanitize regression tests
- 6f7g8h9 docs: operator guide and console help text

Files/areas touched (high level):
- services/runbook-compiler/* (new service)
- services/telemetry-stitcher/* (new worker)
- services/ephemeral-debug-proxy/* (new sandbox proxy)
- web/incident-bot/commands/runbookCompile.ts (command wiring)
- web/console/components/RunbookDraft.vue (UI)
- infra/k8s/runbook-compiler-chart.yaml (helm chart)
- infra/policies/sanitizer-config.yaml (default sanitizer config)
- test/integration/runbook_compiler_smoke_test.go

Testing and CI:
- Unit tests for compilation heuristics and sanitizer rules (coverage: ~86%).
- Integration smoke: runbook compile end-to-end against a local stitcher and a mocked telemetry backend. One intermittent e2e flake (related to external trace service timeout) was retried and passed; we added a retry wrapper to the e2e harness.
- Load test: a small synthetic load harness that validates that the compiler can handle 40 RPS with default batching and falls back gracefully when quota exceeded.

Review conversation summary:
- Priya: Requested clearer failure semantics for the compiler when telemetry is partially available. Action: added a 'degraded' runbook mode and explicit indicators in the output.
- Jonas: Asked for tighter sanitizer controls and audit logs for sanitized artifacts. Action: added audit events and a sanitizer-config change log.
- Evan: Concerned about proxy resource exhaustion. Action: added a global supervisor with circuit-breaker and a maximum replay window config.
- Follow-ups left: add a small linear ticket to instrument cost metrics for runbook compilation (ENG-8622).

Backward compatibility and rollout plan:
- Feature is behind a feature flag (runbook.compiler.enabled = false by default in prod). We will enable in staging for two weeks and run the sampler to compare generated runbooks against existing manual playbooks.
- Canary rollout via the console: 10% of incidents for the paid tier, monitor TTR and false-positive step suggestions.

Operational notes for SRE and security:
- New k8s resources live in the runbook namespace and have poddisruptionbudgets set.
- Sanitizer config is stored in vault path secret/data/incident/sanitizer. Changes to that path trigger an alert to sec-ops.
- We’ve instrumented the following metrics: runbook.compile.duration, stitcher.correlation.count, proxy.replay.count, sanitizer.redactions.count. Dashboards and alerts added in observability-pack repo (PR coming).

Why this is different: Prior PRs focused on snapshot collectors or static playbook hints. This work is the first end-to-end compiler that (1) stitches telemetry slices, (2) emits a prioritized runbook with actionable safe rollbacks, and (3) provides a controlled sandbox to validate remediation steps without touching production state.

Notes for reviewers merging: squash merge; include the merge commit message template that references ENG-8421, ENG-8593. Merged to main after a green CI and approvals from two reviewers.

If anything is unclear or you want to run the sandbox locally, see docs/operators/runbook-compiler.md and the quickstart in services/runbook-compiler/README.md.

title: add-rolling-entropy-degradation-fence
preview:
Summary: Introduces a Rolling Entropy Degradation Fence for canary rollouts. Motivation: percentile-based checks (p50/p95/p99) miss subtle distributional shifts where tails shift gradually or mass moves between latency modes. This PR implements an adaptive entropy-based detector that measures changes in the latency/throughput histogram distribution over a rolling window and fences rollouts when sustained distributional degradation is detected.

Design and behavior: The detector bins observed latency samples into an adaptive histogram (dynamic bucket sizing based on observed percentiles) and computes a rolling Shannon entropy plus a KL-divergence/JS-distance to a baseline histogram. We combine three signals: (1) instantaneous entropy change, (2) KL divergence to baseline, and (3) a sustained drift score computed with exponential decay. A canary fence is tripped only when the composite score exceeds the configured threshold for a configurable duration (default 5 minutes) and when traffic-weighted significance passes a minimal-sample check. The fence triggers: (a) pause rollout (canary traffic frozen), (b) escalate to oncall via alert channel, (c) automatically snapshot recent traces for post-mortem.

Implementation notes: - New module perf_canary/entropy_fence.py implements histogram maintenance, entropy/KL computation, smoothing and scoring. - A lightweight online estimator avoids storing raw samples; uses reservoir sampling for rare-event amplification in tail buckets. - Exposes config knobs via canon canary config: window_seconds, bucket_count, min_samples, entropy_threshold, kl_threshold, sustained_seconds. - Integration with existing rollout-control API: rollout_guard.plug('entropy_fence') returns decisions with reason codes and signal payload for observability. - Dashboard: dashboards/entropy_fence.json added with example panels (entropy timeseries, KL, composite score, sample counts). - Tests: unit + small integration tests under tests/test_entropy_fence.py and a synthetic load test script scripts/load/entropy_sims.py.

Compatibility & ops: Defaults are conservative (low false-positive rate). Gate-added to default canary guard chain but disabled for legacy customers via config flag. No schema changes to persisted canary objects. New IAM role not required.

Risks & mitigations: Entropy can be noisy on low-sample traffic; we added min_samples gating and sample-weighted smoothing. We also include a backoff and auto-unfreeze after manual-confirmation window if team marks event as false-positive.

Testing & validation: Local unit tests + simulated traffic scenarios included. We ran a two-week shadow run on internal staging (release/2026-02 staging) and verified zero false positives on normal traffic, detected 3 injected drift scenarios (gradual tail shift, bimodal shift, traffic-mode flip) and produced actionable signal.

Notes: This approach is complementary to existing percentile and trajectory detectors; recommended to use together for improved sensitivity to silent shifts.

title: Heterogeneous-bandwidth-aware allreduce fanout with KV-shard coalescing and comm/compute overlap
preview:
Motivation: on mixed-cluster deployments we observed long tail latency and suboptimal throughput for tensor-parallel allreduce when nodes have heterogeneous NVLink/intra-node bandwidths or asymmetric cross-node links. Smaller microbatches and low-latency applications (streaming chat) disproportionately hit these tails.

This PR introduces a bandwidth-aware fanout planner and a KV-shard coalescing pass that together reduce cross-node stalls and improve overlap between communication and compute. It also adds a narrow-copy optimized kernel for strided small-chunk merges and a runtime hook that maps comm streams to physical links based on a link-rate profile discovered at startup.

What changed (high level):
- Fanout planner: builds a minimal spanning fanout for allreduce that avoids slow links and increases per-hop concurrency when it detects bandwidth heterogeneity. The planner prefers intra-node NVLink-RDMA pairs, then high-rate cross-node links, and uses low-rate links only as fallback.
- KV-shard coalescer: groups adjacent KV cache shards into aligned coalesced writes to reduce the number of small allgathers. This reduces per-step launch overhead and amortizes DMA for common prefix cache hits.
- Stride-optimized memcpy kernel: a small, vectorized kernel for <4KB strided copies used by the coalescer; reduces memcpy time by up to 3x in microbenchmarks.
- Stream mapper: startup probe collects link bandwidths (via existing topo_probe) and creates stream->link mappings to route collective streams away from congested links.
- Scheduler tweak: a light-weight scheduling hint that encourages launching compute kernels on the stream that finished the local reduce, improving KV reuse and overlap.

Benchmarks: (multi-node, 8xA100-80GB, TP-4)
- Throughput: +18% median for 512-token workloads with batch size 8; +11% median for batch size 2.
- P99 latency: reduced by 24% for streaming microbatch workloads (<=2 microbatches).
- Small allgather count: reduced by 38% for a realistic prefix-caching trace.
- Overhead: startup probe adds ~45ms to init time on first run; negligible once cached.

Compatibility and safety:

text:
Motivation: on mixed-cluster deployments we observed long tail latency and suboptimal throughput for tensor-parallel allreduce when nodes have heterogeneous NVLink/intra-node bandwidths or asymmetric cross-node links. Smaller microbatches and low-latency applications (streaming chat) disproportionately hit these tails.

This PR introduces a bandwidth-aware fanout planner and a KV-shard coalescing pass that together reduce cross-node stalls and improve overlap between communication and compute. It also adds a narrow-copy optimized kernel for strided small-chunk merges and a runtime hook that maps comm streams to physical links based on a link-rate profile discovered at startup.

What changed (high level):
- Fanout planner: builds a minimal spanning fanout for allreduce that avoids slow links and increases per-hop concurrency when it detects bandwidth heterogeneity. The planner prefers intra-node NVLink-RDMA pairs, then high-rate cross-node links, and uses low-rate links only as fallback.
- KV-shard coalescer: groups adjacent KV cache shards into aligned coalesced writes to reduce the number of small allgathers. This reduces per-step launch overhead and amortizes DMA for common prefix cache hits.
- Stride-optimized memcpy kernel: a small, vectorized kernel for <4KB strided copies used by the coalescer; reduces memcpy time by up to 3x in microbenchmarks.
- Stream mapper: startup probe collects link bandwidths (via existing topo_probe) and creates stream->link mappings to route collective streams away from congested links.
- Scheduler tweak: a light-weight scheduling hint that encourages launching compute kernels on the stream that finished the local reduce, improving KV reuse and overlap.

Benchmarks: (multi-node, 8xA100-80GB, TP-4)
- Throughput: +18% median for 512-token workloads with batch size 8; +11% median for batch size 2.
- P99 latency: reduced by 24% for streaming microbatch workloads (<=2 microbatches).
- Small allgather count: reduced by 38% for a realistic prefix-caching trace.
- Overhead: startup probe adds ~45ms to init time on first run; negligible once cached.

Compatibility and safety:
- The fanout planner is opt-in via config flag "runtime.bandwidth_fanout.enabled" (default: true for Dedicated and Private, false for Hosted until canary completes).
- Fallbacks: if topology probe fails or detects homogeneous links, planner falls back to existing balanced tree.
- Unit and integration tests added; new runtime flag is gated behind a feature flag at rollout.

Checklist: 
- [x] Perf microbenchmarks
- [x] Unit tests for planner and coalescer
- [x] E2E smoke on CI
- [x] Docs: added runtime flags and rollout notes
- [x] Bench dashboard entry (Linear ENG-4281)

Related: ENG-4219 (heterogeneous link investigation), ENG-4281 (bench dashboard and canary plan).

Notes: this is intentionally conservative in memory use for the coalescer (max 1.5x temporary buffer). We plan a follow-up to expose a memory/perf knob and to extend the stream mapper to NVSwitch-aware topologies.

title: Smoother autoscaler signal and gateway backpressure for dedicated pools
preview:
Summary: Introduces a smoothing layer in the Dedicated Autoscaler and a lightweight backpressure signal propagated from the gateway to fleet controllers. This reduces autoscaler thrash on spiky traffic and gives gateways a controlled way to shed load gracefully against per-pool soft capacity and throughput guarantees.

Motivation: Customers on Dedicated have reported oscillations when short bursts cross autoscaler thresholds and then recede; this leads to unnecessary GPU spin-ups and billing variance, as well as transient error spikes. The gateway currently responds with 429s or fallback heuristics; we need a first-class, observable mechanism that cooperates with autoscaling and preserves headroom for SLA-critical requests.

Changes in this PR:
- Autoscaler: add Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing of utilization and request rate with configurable half-life per-pool. New config fields: smoothing_window_ms, smoothing_alpha (derived).
- Soft capacity & grace tokens: each pool now tracks a small ephemeral token bucket (grace_tokens) to allow short bursts without triggering scale-up, decaying over time and replenished by underutilization.
- Gateway backpressure: add a new X-Redwood-Backpressure header and a compact gRPC/backchannel (gateway->controller) event to advertise desired ingress rate reductions per-route when pool smoothed_utilization > threshold.
- Controller/gateway handshake: gateway subscribes to per-pool backpressure_quota updates; controllers include backpressure_rate in scheduling decisions and expose metrics.
- Telemetry: new metrics smoothed_utilization, grace_tokens_available, backpressure_rate, backpressure_rejections. Add Prometheus labels for pool_id, model_id, and region.
- API schema: extend dedicated pool spec with smoothing and backpressure knobs; added validation and defaults.
- Console/docs: admin UI now surfaces smoothing window and current backpressure state with guidance.
- Tests: unit tests for smoothing math, integration e2e that injects burst traffic and asserts reduced scale-up churn and controlled 429 behavior.

Checklist:
- [x] Design review with SRE and Runtime teams (ENG-2147)
- [x] Unit tests for smoothing and token bucket
- [x] Integration e2e simulating bursty traffic
- [x] Telemetry dashboards updated
- [x] Migration notes and rollout plan
- [x] Backwards-compatible API schema changes (defaults safe)

Implementation notes:
- EMA parameters chosen to have ~30s half-life by default; pools serving low-latency workloads can set shorter windows.
- Grace tokens default to 2 tokens per pool; a token represents ~1s worth of headroom at pool's nominal throughput.
- Backpressure is advisory: scheduler will attempt to respect backpressure_rate before rejecting; only when backpressure persists will the gateway return 429s.
- To avoid unsafe throttling loops, backpressure updates include a monotonic seq and TTL.

Breaking changes: none expected; all new fields are optional with safe defaults.

Rollout plan: staged rollout: 1) feature gated off by default; 2) enable on Canary Dedicated clusters for 48h; 3) enable for opt-in customers with console toggle; 4) enable by default after 2 weeks of stable metrics.

Migration: API customers can ignore new fields; operators upgrading on-prem should ensure controllers and gateway are updated together.

Testing:
- Unit and integration tests added under tests/e2e/dedicated_smoothing_test.go
- Load test harness reproduces 1-2 minute burst profiles and asserts scale-up events reduced by >=50% while 95th-p99 latency impact <5% in our baseline.

- 0f9a8b2: controller: add smoothing math and metrics
- 1b3d2c4: gateway: backpressure header + backchannel client
- 8a7f6e1: api: add dedicated pool schema changes + validation
- c3e4d5b: telemetry: register new metrics and dashboards
- d4f2a9f: console: show per-pool smoothing/backpressure state
- f5b6c7d: tests: unit + e2e harness for burst scenarios

Files changed (high level):
- controller/autoscaler/smoothing.go: implement EMA and smoothing helpers (approx. 220 LOC)
- pkg/allocator/soft_capacity.go: grace tokens and soft capacity decisions
- gateway/backpressure/handler.go: header parsing and backchannel emitter
- api/config/v1/dedicated_pool.yaml: schema additions and docs
- telemetry/metrics_registry.go: new metric registration
- console/src/pages/pool/BackpressureCard.tsx: UI surface
- tests/e2e/dedicated_smoothing_test.go: load test harness
- helm/charts/redwood/templates/pool-config.yaml: default knobs
- ops/rollout/2026-03-dedicated-smoothing.md: rollout instructions

Small illustrative snippets:
- smoothing.go (EMA core): "smoothed = alpha*instant + (1-alpha)*smoothed"
- handler.go (gateway header): "if hdr := req.Header.Get(\"X-Redwood-Backpressure\"); hdr != \"\" { parseAndApply(hdr) }"

Review thread summary:

- Leah Kim (Runtime): asked for benchmark numbers for default EMA half-life; Response: added results from synthetic bursts showing 60% reduction in scale-ups with 30s half-life.
- Owen Patel (Security): requested audit logging for backpressure events; Response: added structured audit logs under telemetry/backpressure_events.
- Reviewer approvals: Marco Alvarez approved after TTL addition; Leah Kim approved after benchmark numbers; Owen Patel approved after audit logs.

Merge and post-merge actions:

- Updated canary dashboards and set alert: DedicatedPool.SmoothedUtilization > 95% for 5m -> page oncall

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: misconfigured smoothing window could delay needed scale-ups -> mitigation: default conservative half-life and admin override to disable smoothing per pool.
- Risk: backpressure loops between gateway and controller -> mitigation: monotonic seq + TTL + min-scheduling floor.

Release notes: "Improves autoscaler stability by smoothing utilization signals and introduces an advisory backpressure mechanism from gateway to controllers to reduce churn on short bursts. Operators: feature is opt-in and has safe defaults; see admin docs for tuning guidance."

title: Multiplexer: parallel tool execution, idempotent retries, and ergonomic result injection
preview:
Motivation: production workloads increasingly issue multiple tool calls per response (lookups, enrichment, external actions) and clients expect predictable injection semantics, low-latency parallelism, and transparent retry behavior when external tools fail. This PR introduces a lightweight multiplexer for tool execution that: 1) executes independent tool calls in parallel when safe, 2) exposes an explicit idempotency token + retry middleware for transient tool failures, 3) provides an ergonomic result-injection API surface for handlers (deterministic injection points with ordering hints), and 4) keeps OpenAI-compatibility behaviors (function-calling fallback and structured outputs) intact.

Summary of changes: commits implement a runtime-level ToolMultiplexer that accepts a list of ToolCall requests and returns deterministic ToolResults with stable ids and insertion points. We added a RetryMiddleware that respects per-tool idempotency tokens and exponential backoff policies, and a backpressure-aware worker pool that bounds concurrency based on route config. The compatibility shim for openai->redwood function calling was updated to translate function return values into the new ToolResult shape. Documentation and SDK ergonomics were updated (Python and TS examples) and end-to-end tests were added for mixed success/failure scenarios.

title: bake signed artifact index and provenance into private-installer release manifests
preview: Motivation: private deployments (VPC/on-prem) require a reproducible, signed index that bundles release artifacts (installer tarballs, Helm charts, Terraform modules), attestations (SBOM/prov.json), and signature metadata so customers can verify what they deploy. This PR introduces a baked 'signed-artifact-index' generation step in the private-installer release pipeline, integrates Notary v2-compatible signing (via GCP KMS in CI), surfaces provenance files alongside Helm and Terraform artifacts, and wires the installer/upgrade tooling to consume the index during installs and rollbacks. Implementation summary: 1) CI: add .github/workflows/release-private-installer-index.yml that builds artifacts in a hermetic container, generates SBOMs (syft), produces provenance JSON, and invokes notary-v2 client to produce signatures and an OCI index blob that contains pointers to signed artifacts; 2) release manifests: produce release-manifests/private/<version>/signed-artifact-index.json (index is content-addressed and references OCI descriptors for artifacts plus prov and sbom); 3) installer: private-installer/installer/manifest_fetcher.go updated to fetch and verify the signed index using a configurable verification key/cert bundle; 4) helm & terraform: helm-charts/values.yaml and terraform/modules/private_release/main.tf updated to prefer signed artifacts when index is present; 5) upgrade/rollback tooling: upgrade-planer now reads index history to plan deterministic rollbacks. Checklist: - [x] CI job builds artifacts and SBOMs - [x] Notary v2 signing step implemented with KMS key reference (kept configurable per org) - [x] Signed index JSON schema and example added - [x] Installer verification path implemented and tested locally - [x] Terraform module fallback added when index absent - [x] Documentation and upgrade notes drafted (docs/private-release-index.md) Notes on security: private key material is never stored in the repo; CI uses ephemeral signing via KMS grant. For air-gapped/private builds, the index generator supports an 'offline-sign' mode where prov + sbom are exported for customer-side signing. Rollout plan: land on main behind feature flag RELEASE_SIGNED_INDEX, enable on canary private release, collect verified installs, then flip default for new releases. Related: follows up on ENG-4920 and ties into SUP-3212 customer request for verifiable artifacts. Release notes: see release_notes field.

title: introduce-retry-bundles-and-stream-progress-telemetry-across-sdks
preview: Context: Customers have asked for a compact, declarative way to reuse retry semantics across SDKs and to receive lightweight progress telemetry for long-running streaming requests. This PR introduces (1) retry bundles — named retry/profile presets that can be referenced by "retryPolicy" on client config and (2) a stream-progress telemetry envelope emitted by the SDKs for long-running pull/push streams. Motivation: reduce duplicated retry configuration in apps and enable observability consumers (SLO dashboards, SRE) to surface stream progress without instrumenting app-level callbacks. Summary of changes: added a retry bundle registry and lookup time resolution in the client core; exposed a small opt-in "stream_progress" event channel that SDKs can emit at stable intervals (configurable); implemented consistent semantics across Python/TypeScript/Go; added unit and integration tests and bumped SDK versions. Checklist: - [x] Design doc: linear/ENG-3245 (linked) - [x] Cross-SDK API proposal reviewed by SDK leads - [x] Unit tests for registry lookup and fallback - [x] Integration tests on hosted-compat test harness - [x] Version bumps and changelogs updated - [x] E2E smoke in canary environment (observability) API compatibility: The client constructors accept an optional new field `retry_profile` where callers can pass either a string (bundle name) or an inline profile object. The default remains unchanged unless `retry_profile` is provided. The `stream_progress` channel is opt-in via `enable_stream_progress=true` in client config; when enabled the SDK emits JSON-serializable events that include a monotonic `offset_estimate`, `tokens_emitted`, and `elapsed_ms`. Commits summary: - Add retry-bundle registry, resolution and small DSL for preset values - Wire retry lookup into request pipeline and backoff scheduler - Add stream-progress envelope, interval runner, and config knobs in Py/TS/Go - Add unit tests and integration harness changes - Bump package versions and changelogs Changed files (high level): sdks/python/redwood/client.py, sdks/python/redwood/retry_bundles.py, sdks/python/tests/test_retry_bundles.py, sdks/typescript/src/client.ts, sdks/typescript/src/stream_progress.ts, sdks/typescript/test/retry-bundles.test.ts, sdks/go/client/retry_registry.go, sdks/go/stream/progress.go, docs/sdk/retry-profiles.md Testing notes: - Initial CI run failed lint in TS due to export ordering; fixed in commit 3. - Integration runs on the hosted-canary revealed a timing edge where stream-progress events could be too frequent for very high-throughput streams; added a min-interval clamp and made the interval adaptive. Backwards compatibility and migration: No breaking changes. Existing callers not opting into `retry_profile` or `enable_stream_progress` see no change. Security: retry bundles are client-only; no new server-side surface is exposed. Observability: telemetry envelope follows semantic conventions in docs/sdk/telemetry.md. Related work: touches ENG-3301 (streaming-observability) and SUP-1182 (customer support case requesting standard retry presets).

title: Introduce token-lifecycle burnup histogram, cross-service latency fingerprint, and alert coalescer
preview:
Context: token-level SLO noise and long-tail cost/latency alerts are creating alert fatigue. Existing token-cost rollups capture per-token aggregates but struggle to: (1) show how cost accumulates across pipeline stages (ingest, model, postprocess), (2) identify cross-service latency shape changes (same total latency but different phase breakdown), and (3) suppress repeated similar alerts that indicate the same incident.

What this PR does: introduces three coordinated pieces:
1) token_lifecycle_burnup histogram metric (metric/token_lifecycle_burnup): records cumulative token cost/latency at stage boundaries per invocation (labels: tenant_id, model, route, stage). Supports configurable buckets and exported rollups.
2) latency_fingerprint: a small vectorized signature (string) derived from percentile deltas across stages for an invocation; stored as a tag on traces and emitted as a low-cardinality rollup for fast grouping.
3) alert_coalescer: a server-side component in observability-alerts that groups token-level alerts using latency_fingerprint similarity and recent-window dedupe rules (window default 5m, jittered). Coalescer emits a single incident with aggregated counts and exemplar trace links.

Design notes:
- token_lifecycle_burnup is implemented as a histogram with predefined exponential buckets plus explicit tail buckets to capture heavy-tailed token cost. The SDK helper is backwards-compatible (adds a single call at stage boundaries).

- Feature flag: observability.token_burnup.enabled=false by default.
- Canary: enable for 2 enterprise tenants for 48 hours, sampling rate 5% invocations.

text:
Context: token-level SLO noise and long-tail cost/latency alerts are creating alert fatigue. Existing token-cost rollups capture per-token aggregates but struggle to: (1) show how cost accumulates across pipeline stages (ingest, model, postprocess), (2) identify cross-service latency shape changes (same total latency but different phase breakdown), and (3) suppress repeated similar alerts that indicate the same incident.

What this PR does: introduces three coordinated pieces:
1) token_lifecycle_burnup histogram metric (metric/token_lifecycle_burnup): records cumulative token cost/latency at stage boundaries per invocation (labels: tenant_id, model, route, stage). Supports configurable buckets and exported rollups.
2) latency_fingerprint: a small vectorized signature (string) derived from percentile deltas across stages for an invocation; stored as a tag on traces and emitted as a low-cardinality rollup for fast grouping.
3) alert_coalescer: a server-side component in observability-alerts that groups token-level alerts using latency_fingerprint similarity and recent-window dedupe rules (window default 5m, jittered). Coalescer emits a single incident with aggregated counts and exemplar trace links.

title: KernelTide contention visualizer and probabilistic warmup coordinator for stability-focused bench suites
preview:
Motivation: We've observed non-deterministic p95/p99 spikes in a subset of long-running throughput benchmarks that correlate with kernel-level contention and uneven warmup behavior across workers. This PR introduces two complementary components: (1) KernelTide — a contention visualizer that aggregates per-kernel occupancy, stall-reasons and scheduling collisions into time-aligned heatmaps and compact contention-curves; (2) ProbabilisticWarmupCoordinator — a controller that runs randomized preflight primer passes across cohorts to probabilistically cover different KV cache and memory-placement states before measurement windows.

What this PR does (summary): Adds a lightweight sampling probe that annotates kernel events in the benchmark worker (Linux perf + runtime hooks), a postprocessor that synthesizes per-request kernel timelines into a compact parquet-friendly schema, an interactive visualizer (static HTML + JS) for heatmaps and contour plots, and a coordinator that schedules warmup primers with configurable coverage confidence and early-stop heuristics. Integrates with existing bench orchestration and the CI regression pipeline so nightly runs produce kernel-contour artifacts and the stable-run coordinator reduces variance for p95/p99 assertions.

Why this is different: Existing tools produce flamegraphs and token-latency histograms, but none expose the time-correlated kernel-contention signal mapped back to sequence phase and KV-cache state. KernelTide surfaces short-lived contention modes that cause ephemeral long-tail spikes. The ProbabilisticWarmupCoordinator reduces false-positive regressions by ensuring a statistically representative warmup set prior to capture windows.

Compatibility and runbook: Backwards compatible with existing bench manifests. To opt-in add "kerneltide: true" to a workload stanza; warmup coordinator config is optional and defaults to conservative settings. New artifacts are stored under artifacts/kerneltide/{run_id} with a _contours.parquet file and an index.json. README includes visualization usage and troubleshooting steps.

Notes on measurement: We ran the stability suite against a 16-node mini-cluster (mixed A100/V100) with synthetic seq-mix and real prompt traces; KernelTide flagged a scheduler thrash on one node that correlated with a p99 increase of ~18%. After applying the coordinator, p99 variance decreased 34% across 30 runs. Full benchmark logs and the reproduce script are attached in the PR files.

Related: ENG-8342 (noise reduction workstream), ENG-8401 (observability exports), SUP-231 (customer incident that prompted deeper per-kernel analysis).
