title: Add stratified noise calibrator and adaptive probe scheduler for low-signal canaries
preview: Context: Our canary detector has been producing noisy alerts for low-volume / heterogenous traffic slices (short queries, long queries, browser vs API) leading to unnecessary rollbacks. Motivation: reduce false positives and speed up meaningful detection in low-signal cohorts without increasing probe cost. This PR introduces two complementary components: (1) Stratified Noise Calibrator (SNC) — a per-stratum robust noise estimator that derives baseline variance and Minimal Detectable Effect (MDE) using median-absolute-deviation + IQR trimming and an empirical noise model; and (2) Adaptive Probe Scheduler (APS) — a probe allocation layer that dynamically assigns probe budget to strata based on expected information gain (posterior variance reduction) and the user's rollout priority hints. Approach: SNC computes noise baselines per stratum (user_cohort, model_variant, sequence_length_bucket) and emits metrics (perf_canary.noise_baseline, perf_canary.mde_estimate). APS queries SNC and current traffic rates, computes expected value-of-information, then increments probe rates for strata where MDE > configured detection target or where posterior uncertainty dominates. Both pieces are integrated into the rollout guard: when aggregate confidence is below threshold, APS will increase probes up to a capped budget and, if uncertainty persists, the guard will hold or throttle the canary. Statistical protection: SNC applies Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across strata to control multi-comparison false alarms and exposes a confidence score to the guard. Implementation notes: core logic implemented in Go in go/perf/canary; metrics and alerts via Prometheus rules; simulator and integration scenarios in py/tools/canary_simulator to reproduce 0.5%–5% regressions per cohort. Tests: unit tests for SNC math, APS allocation policy, and a small-scale end-to-end simulation that runs in CI (simulated traffic with deterministic RNG seeds). Backwards compatibility: default behavior is off; rollout policy must opt-in via canary.config.alpha.enable_stratified_calibrator = true. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-5421) [x] unit tests [x] integration simulator scenarios [x] Prometheus recording rules and alert templates [x] docs and runbook [x] migration notes for existing canary dashboards. Risks & mitigations: metric cardinality — strata keys are canonicalized and capped to configured cardinality; noisy short-lived cohorts — APS applies minimum probe windows to avoid churn. Open questions: tune default MDE multiplier for PLG customers (left conservative).

title: Add latency-cliff detector and adaptive-concurrency rollout guard
preview: Motivation: We've observed hard 'latency cliff' failure modes where p95/p99 jump sharply within a short window even though rolling averages look stable; these events bypass percentile-based canaries and lead to partial outages. This PR implements a latency-cliff detector that detects rapid percentile deltas across short windows and wires an adaptive-concurrency rollout guard that temporarily reduces per-replica concurrency and pauses rollouts when a cliff is detected. Changes include detector algorithm, integration with the canary evaluation pipeline, a lightweight local load harness for reproducible cliffs, dashboards, and unit/integration tests. Checklist: [x] detector algorithm and unit tests, [x] canary integration and gating, [x] adaptive-concurrency policy and actuator, [x] dashboards and runbook, [x] CI flake fixes. Rollout plan: rollout as a guarded feature flag for 2 weeks, enable in perf-canary staging, monitor metrics and KB/alerts, then enable for canary->prod rollouts. Backwards compatibility: no public API changes; the guard is opt-in via canary config; defaults are off. Performance cost: detector is O(1) per incoming metric update and uses bounded memory. Related: ENG-1428, ENG-1534. Test plan: unit tests cover algorithm edge cases; integration test injects synthetic cliff using examples/load-profiles/stepped-cliff.yaml; manual verification with dashboard in charts/perf-canary-dashboard.json.

title: crosslang-credential-sanity-and-stream-handoff-polish
preview:
Motivation: We observed intermittent duplicate credential refreshes and subtle stream-resume races when long-lived streaming consumers migrated credentials (lease rotations) in parallel across the three official SDKs. This PR harmonizes credential lifecycles and clarifies the handoff contract for pull/push-style streams so that SDKs exhibit consistent, observable behavior under rotation and network flakiness.

What this PR does (high level):
- Introduces a small, language-agnostic contract: streams must surface a stable 'lease_id' with every chunk boundary; consumers can request a graceful handoff by calling Stream.handoff() which yields an idempotent checkpoint token. This lets client code coordinate credentials and checkpoints atomically.
- Adds a compact backoff/retry preset called "sdk:stream-sane" (adaptive jitter + capped linear) and wires it into all SDKs as an opt-in default for streaming endpoints.
- Centralizes credential refresh orchestration logic in the redwood/clients layer so that the same refresh policy drives Python/TS/Go wrappers used by the platform build; reduces duplicated timers and race surface.
- Implements ergonomic API shims per SDK: Python adds Stream.handoff_checkpoint(checkpoint), TypeScript adds stream.handoff() returning a small token, and Go exposes Stream.ConfirmHandoff(token) which is safe to call idempotently.
- Bumps packaged SDK versions consumed by the monorepo: python-client v0.9.8, typescript-client v2.1.0, go-client v1.5.4.

Files changed (high level):
- clients/credential/orchestrator.py (new): orchestrator that serializes refresh and exposes a lightweight lease registry for in-process consumers.
- clients/js/streaming/handoff.ts (new): handoff helper and typed token.
- clients/go/stream/handoff.go (new): idempotent confirmation helper and small tests.
- sdk-bindings/python/redwood_sdk/stream.py (+docs): Stream.handoff_checkpoint API + docstrings + tests.
- sdk-bindings/ts/src/stream.ts (+docs): stream.handoff() API + tests.
- sdk-bindings/go/stream_test.go: unit tests covering race scenarios.
- e2e-tests/streaming/credential_rotation_test.py: end-to-end that mimics lease rotation during active streams.
- infra/ci/sdk-version-sync.yml: pipeline step to verify package bumps and publish-ready artifacts.

Checklist:
- [x] Cross-language design doc reviewed (ENG-1429)
- [x] Added unit tests for each SDK variant
- [x] Added an e2e test that reproduces the previous flakey scenario and shows deterministic handoff
- [x] CI green on all platforms (linux/mac/windows where applicable)
- [x] Release notes drafted

Testing and validation notes:
- Unit tests cover the main race (concurrent refresh + stream chunk emission) and validate that at-most-one refresh is in-flight per lease_id.
- E2E test runs a churn scenario (1000 short-lived streams + 5 rotating leases) in CI's integration runner and asserts no duplicate refresh attempts and no dropped final chunks.
- We validated memory footprint and timer counts across Python/TS/Go; the orchestrator reduces active timers by ~60% in the in-process client case.

Backward compatibility and migration:
- Existing stream consumers continue to work; the handoff APIs are additive. If a consumer does not call the new handoff helpers, the underlying orchestrator falls back to the previous non-deterministic refresh path for compatibility.
- The small semantic clarification (lease_id visibility at chunk boundaries) is documented; it only affects consumers that rely on implicit ordering across refreshes.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge to main behind a feature flag (enabled for SDK nightly builds).
2) Monitor telemetry: stream error rate, refresh attempts per minute, and tail-latency.
3) Flip default for new SDK builds and then enable the orchestrator in Redwood Hosted gradually.

Related tickets: ENG-1429 (design), ENG-1430 (e2e validation), PM-331 (customer report).

Commits (summary):
- 9c1aa5a: Add credential orchestrator and lease registry (clients/credential/orchestrator.py)
- b7f2d34: Expose Stream.handoff_checkpoint in python SDK + tests
- e08a3e1: Add TS stream handoff helper and types (clients/js/streaming/handoff.ts)
- 3f6b2c2: Implement go confirm handoff and add deterministic tests
- 5a4c0bd: Wire sdk version bumps and update changelogs
- da7e9ff: e2e test reproducer for lease rotation race

CI summary:
- linux-unit: PASS
- windows-unit: PASS
- mac-unit: PASS
- integration/stream-e2e: PASS (5m job)
- lint: PASS (minor style fixes applied)
- publish-check: PASS

Merge notes:
- Merged via squash to main. Author performed a manual local smoke on macOS and linux runner before final merge. The PR required one round of changes after initial review to tighten the TS types and eliminate a goroutine leak in the Go handoff helper.

Observed follow-ups (post-merge):
- Telemetry: decreased duplicate-refresh metric by 98% in nightly runs.
- Ops: Raised a small doc tweak to clarify token lifetime for long-lived streams (follow-up PR queued).

If you want to exercise the APIs locally:
- Python: from redwood_sdk import Stream; token = stream.handoff_checkpoint(my_checkpoint)
- TypeScript: const token = await stream.handoff(); // pass to server-side confirm flow
- Go: tok := stream.Handoff(); stream.ConfirmHandoff(tok)

title: Add DMA stall & kernel-latency counters with sigprof-compatible timeline exporter
preview:
Context: We need finer-grained, low-overhead indicators of where inference stalls occur inside the serving runtime so that model routing, batching and kernel-selection heuristics can be tuned without attaching heavy profilers. This PR adds DMA-stall counters collected from the GPU driver telemetry shim, per-kernel latency histograms sampled into buckets, and a compact timeline exporter that emits SigProf-compatible records that downstream dashboards (and our internal perfboard) can ingest. Motivation: several customer incidents pointed to intermittent pauses that didn't show up in token-level latency traces (ENG-1223). Existing CPU-side sampling catches high-level stalls but misses kernel queuing and DMA scheduling hotspots.

Summary of changes (high level):
- runtime/telemetry: new dma_counters.{h,cc} implementing low-overhead atomic counters for DMA queue stalls and preemption events.
- runtime/profiler: kernel_latency_histogram, continuous bucketing with sliding-window reset and exponential decay.
- tools/timeline_exporter: sigprof_exporter that converts our internal samples to a compact record stream (.sprec) compatible with SigProf importers.
- benchmarks/microbench: added microbench harness that simulates multi-model mixed workloads and emits representative traces for dashboard ingestion.
- docs/observability: brief guidance and sample Grafana dashboard JSON for the latency buckets and DMA-stall heatmap.

Commits (summary):
1) Add dma counter primitives and basic increment paths (mchen)
2) Wire counter increments in queueing/scheduler path and kernel dispatch (mchen)
3) Implement kernel_latency_histogram with lockless circular buffers (mchen)
4) Add sigprof-compatible exporter and small CLI tool to emit .sprec from live socket (mchen)
5) Microbench harness + example dashboard JSON (mchen)
6) Tests, comments, CI adjustments and small flake fixes (mchen)

Files changed (representative):
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.h
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.cc
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.h
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.cc
- runtime/scheduler/scheduler_dispatch.cc (insert increments at dispatch path, ~12 LOC)
- tools/timeline_exporter/sigprof_exporter.cc
- benchmarks/microbench/sigprof_dmamicro.cxx
- docs/observability/sigprof-dma-dashboard.json
- tests/telemetry/test_dma_counters.cc

Small illustrative diff (scheduler hook):
- // before: dispatch kernel
+ // after: increment DMA stall counter if queue detected busy
+ dma_counters::global()->maybe_increment_dma_stall(queue_id);

Design notes and tradeoffs: we intentionally use a very small number of atomic counters and compact histogram buckets to keep per-request overhead sub-200ns on hot paths. The histogram is sampled on kernel completion (not per token) to avoid touching hot token paths. We opted for SigProf record compatibility instead of perfetto to broaden downstream tool options and because SigProf's compact record model reduces exporter overhead.

Bench results (CI microbench + local runs):
- Baseline (no counters): median dispatch latency 74us, p99 1.9ms
- With counters + exporter enabled (sampling only): median dispatch latency 77us (+4%), p99 2.0ms (+5%)
- Continuous microbench (mixed sequence lengths) shows DMA-stall counter correlation with p99 spikes in the kernel queue, enabling reliable detection in dashboards.

Test coverage: unit tests for counter increment/decrement and histogram bucketing. Integration test that runs microbench and verifies exporter produces valid .sprec frames and dashboard import script accepts them.

Review thread (high level):
- Liam: requested stronger guarantees about lockless buffer memory reclamation. Author replied with a short RFC link and added a single-writer circular buffer design and explicit hazard pointer comment.
- Priya: asked for clearer docs on how to enable exporter in Dedicated vs Private deployments. Author added docs/observability section and a note about disabling exporter by default (env var REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=0).
- Ethan: ran local scale test and flagged a spurious flake in the microbench; author fixed timing window and re-ran CI.

text:
Context: We need finer-grained, low-overhead indicators of where inference stalls occur inside the serving runtime so that model routing, batching and kernel-selection heuristics can be tuned without attaching heavy profilers. This PR adds DMA-stall counters collected from the GPU driver telemetry shim, per-kernel latency histograms sampled into buckets, and a compact timeline exporter that emits SigProf-compatible records that downstream dashboards (and our internal perfboard) can ingest. Motivation: several customer incidents pointed to intermittent pauses that didn't show up in token-level latency traces (ENG-1223). Existing CPU-side sampling catches high-level stalls but misses kernel queuing and DMA scheduling hotspots.

Summary of changes (high level):
- runtime/telemetry: new dma_counters.{h,cc} implementing low-overhead atomic counters for DMA queue stalls and preemption events.
- runtime/profiler: kernel_latency_histogram, continuous bucketing with sliding-window reset and exponential decay.
- tools/timeline_exporter: sigprof_exporter that converts our internal samples to a compact record stream (.sprec) compatible with SigProf importers.
- benchmarks/microbench: added microbench harness that simulates multi-model mixed workloads and emits representative traces for dashboard ingestion.
- docs/observability: brief guidance and sample Grafana dashboard JSON for the latency buckets and DMA-stall heatmap.

Commits (summary):
1) Add dma counter primitives and basic increment paths (mchen)
2) Wire counter increments in queueing/scheduler path and kernel dispatch (mchen)
3) Implement kernel_latency_histogram with lockless circular buffers (mchen)
4) Add sigprof-compatible exporter and small CLI tool to emit .sprec from live socket (mchen)
5) Microbench harness + example dashboard JSON (mchen)
6) Tests, comments, CI adjustments and small flake fixes (mchen)

Files changed (representative):
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.h
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.cc
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.h
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.cc
- runtime/scheduler/scheduler_dispatch.cc (insert increments at dispatch path, ~12 LOC)
- tools/timeline_exporter/sigprof_exporter.cc
- benchmarks/microbench/sigprof_dmamicro.cxx
- docs/observability/sigprof-dma-dashboard.json
- tests/telemetry/test_dma_counters.cc

Small illustrative diff (scheduler hook):
- // before: dispatch kernel
+ // after: increment DMA stall counter if queue detected busy
+ dma_counters::global()->maybe_increment_dma_stall(queue_id);

title: Contextual Hypothesis Writer and Sandboxed Fix Prover
preview:
Context and motivation:
This PR adds two coordinated features to the incident-bot suite: (1) a Contextual Hypothesis Writer that synthesizes prioritized remediation hypotheses from alert telemetry and runbook fragments, and (2) a Sandboxed Fix Prover that attempts safe, read-only or reversible dry-run fixes in ephemeral sandboxes to validate hypotheses before recommending operator action. The goal is to reduce mean time to mitigation for operational incidents while keeping all experiments isolated and auditable.

High-level design and behavior:
- Hypothesis Writer consumes the incident snapshot (logs, traces, metrics, recent deploys, route maps) and runbook snippets to propose 2-5 ranked hypotheses with rationale and confidence score. It attaches minimal reproduction hints and a short sequence of commands or API calls required to validate the hypothesis.
- Fix Prover executes validation workflows in ephemeral sandboxes with strict resource/time limits and capability restrictions. Each prover run produces a non-destructive evidence bundle: commands executed, API call traces, snapshots of pre/post read-only state where applicable, and a safety verdict (validated / not validated / inconclusive).
- Communication model: incident-bot UI shows hypotheses inline with a "Validate in Sandbox" action. Sandbox runs are gated behind RBAC; only users with the prover:run role can trigger them. All sandbox runs are recorded and retained for audit.

Commits summary:
- 8f3a2d5: initial hypothesis writer core + ranking model glue (feature flags for rollout)
- bc91e4b: add runbook fragment linker and heuristics for metric-to-action mapping
- 2cd9f44: sandboxed prover framework (k8s ephemeral job runner + policy engine)
- f7b0a77: prover safety policies (capability whitelists, syscall filter shim, timeouts)
- 9a2b1e3: UI wiring: validate button, status flow, and audit view
- d4a0c3f: tests (unit + sandbox integration tests) and docs

Files / areas changed (high-level):
- cmd/incident-bot/handlers/hypothesis_writer.go (new)
- internal/sandbox/prover/* (new framework + policy configs)
- ui/components/HypothesisCard.tsx (UI hooks and button)
- integrations/k8s/ephemeral_runner.yaml (deployment template with resource limits)
- docs/oncall/hypothesis-generator.md (new operator docs)
- test/e2e/sandbox_prover_test.go (integration coverage)

Security and safety considerations addressed:
- Prover runs are strictly read-only by default; write-capable checkers require explicit feature flag and PM sign-off.
- All external command execution goes through a syscall-filtering shim + seccomp profile; network egress is restricted to internal API endpoints only.
- RBAC enforced at API layer and audited to the events timeline.
- Sensitive fields in evidence bundles are redacted by a sanitizer before storage. Redaction rules are configurable.

Testing and CI notes:
- Initial CI run had a failing integration test due to intermittent namespace cleanup; added retry+cleanup in test harness (see commit d4a0c3f).
- Security static analysis flagged uses of temporary file creation in prover; updated to use atomic temp APIs and cleared file handles on exit.
- Final CI matrix: unit (pass), linter (pass), e2e sandbox (pass after test harness fix).

Review conversation (summary):
- Owen Park: "Great direction — please show the RBAC surface and how this will look to SOC. Also add audit retention knobs."
  - Author: "Added RBAC docs, retention knobs default to 90d, made retention configurable in operator config (docs updated)."
- Lena Ho: "Sandboxing looks reasonable, but we need explicit proof that no credentials can be leaked via environment variables."
  - Author: "Added environment scrubbing and an explicit test case that injects faux credentials and asserts they are not present in evidence bundles."
- Marcelo D'Souza (security): "Rejecting any run that would result in outbound access to external internet domains; please add egress policy."
  - Author: "Implemented network policy to restrict egress to internal clusters only and added a policy test (k8s network policy)."
- Priya Natarajan: "Integration tests flaked once — please ensure cleanup does not leave stale namespaces."
  - Author: "Fixed with retries and deterministic namespace naming; added a post-test sweep job in CI."

Checklist performed before merge:
- [x] Unit tests for hypothesis ranking heuristics
- [x] Integration tests for prover sandbox lifecycle
- [x] Security review (Marcelo) and RBAC mapping
- [x] Documentation for on-call usage and opt-in rollout steps
- [x] Audit logging and retention config added
- [x] Feature flag gating (incidents.enableHypothesisWriter, incidents.enableProver)

Rollout plan:
- Step 1 (canary, 2% of tenants): enable Hypothesis Writer only (no prover) to gather feedback on hypothesis quality; monitor precision/false positives via eval prompts.
- Step 2 (internal org only): enable prover for engineers in dev orgs to exercise sandboxes.
- Step 3 (gradual): open to SRE teams once sandbox metrics (latency, resource usage, safety verdict rates) are stable.

Release notes:
- Adds a Contextual Hypothesis Writer that proposes ranked remediation hypotheses for incidents and a Sandboxed Fix Prover that validates hypotheses in ephemeral, auditable sandboxes. Feature flagged and gated by RBAC.

Merge details:
- Merged via squash on 2026-02-25 after all reviewers approved. CI green on final run.

Follow-ups / TODOs:
- Add cost accounting hooks for sandbox runs to show token/resource cost attribution (ENG-4838).
- Add a configurable approval flow for write-capable provers behind an enterprise compliance toggle.
- Improve hypothesis ranking model with additional signal sources (deploy diffs, config drift).

title: Introduce jurisdictional operator claims, KMS proxy with region affinity, and audit canonicalizer
preview: Motivation: operator-initiated requests that used fallback KMS routes could violate tenant residency expectations in multi-geozone deployments. This PR introduces a lightweight KMS proxy with region-affinity routing and a jurisdictional operator-claim propagation middleware that ties RBAC v2 decisions to geozone claims. It also adds an audit header canonicalizer to ensure consistent downstream proofs for compliance ingestion. Changes include a claim backfill migration for existing operator sessions and telemetry for residency decision sampling. Checklist: - [x] design doc and Linear ticket ENG-4521 linked, - [x] RBAC v2 policy and policy tests, - [x] KMS proxy and unit tests, - [x] migration/backfill job and smoke tests, - [x] staged rollout flag + installer hook, - [ ] manual staging canary and runbook signoff. Design references: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4521 and internal design note https://github.com/redwood/internal/designs/kms-region-affinity.md. Rollout plan: feature gate on by default in staging, opt-in via installer flag for dedicated/private customers while telemetry is monitored for 72h before full enablement.

title: add-siesta-presubmit-prober-and-dependency-visibility-index
preview:
Context:
We have growing CI wall time from running monolithic integration matrices for small changes. This PR introduces a lightweight presubmit prober (codename: Siesta) and a dependency visibility index to reduce premerge feedback time. The prober is intentionally minimal: static-source heuristics, quick-path test selectors, and a small Go probe binary that emits a deterministic 'presubmit fingerprint' for changed paths.

Motivation:
- Reduce developer wait times on common doc/SDK/infra tweaks by avoiding full integration runs unless needed.
- Provide a reproducible preflight check that can be run locally and in CI to gate expensive shards.
- Make dependency visibility explicit so presubmit selection is conservatively correct for transitive changes.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds a new lightweight Go binary cmd/presubmit-prober (approx 120 LOC) that scans a change list and computes a presubmit fingerprint and a list of impacted test tags.
- Adds a dependency-visibility index stored as a small YAML under infra/dep-index.yaml (generated by scripts/dep-indexer). Index is used by the prober to expand impact beyond direct file diffs.
- Introduces a new CI job in .github/workflows/preflight-siesta.yml that runs the prober and conditionally triggers downstream CI lanes via workflow_dispatch with annotated tags.
- Refactors the existing presubmit orchestration script (scripts/presubmit-orchestrator.sh) to call the prober and short-circuit heavy shards when not needed.
- Adds unit tests for the prober and a small e2e harness that validates tag selection against a few representative diffs in tests/fixtures.

Design notes:
- The prober output is deterministic and signed by CI using a job metadata checksum to avoid accidental cache poisoning.
- Dependency index is intentionally conservative (over-approximate) and is regenerated via CI schedule weekly and during major dependency upgrades. The generator lives at scripts/dep-indexer/ and is separate from runtime prober to keep presubmit fast.
- Rollout is gated: new CI workflow runs in parallel but does not yet prevent merges. We will flip the enforcement guard in a follow-up after 1 week of telemetry.

Testing and results:
- Local unit tests added; e2e harness simulates 6 change sets (docs-only, sdk-typo, runtime-scheduler, kvcache tweak, quantization profile change, infra/helm tweak).
- On the repo's mid-sized change set sample, presubmit wall time reduced ~38% for non-runtime changes when prober short-circuits heavy shards.

Migration / rollout checklist:
- [x] Add prober and dep-index generator (this PR).
- [x] Run weekly index generation scheduled job (CI schedule added).
- [x] Monitor presubmit hit/miss rates and errors for 7 days.
- [x] Flip enforcement to block merges if prober indicates a heavy change (follow-up PR).

Notes for reviewers:
- Focus review on presubmit-prober deterministic logic and dep-index schema. The generator is conservative by design; correctness favors over-inclusion.
- I intentionally kept the Go binary minimal and dependency-free (std lib only) to avoid CI image bloat.

Commits (high-level):
- feat(presubmit): add cmd/presubmit-prober (initial implementation and tests)
- feat(ci): add preflight-siesta workflow and integrate with presubmit-orchestrator
- feat(infra): add scripts/dep-indexer and infra/dep-index.yaml (seed index)
- refactor(presubmit): update presubmit-orchestrator to call prober
- test: add fixtures and e2e harness for prober behavior

Changed areas (high level):
- cmd/presubmit-prober/* (new)
- scripts/dep-indexer/* (new)
- infra/dep-index.yaml (new, ~2.1KB seed)
- .github/workflows/preflight-siesta.yml (new CI workflow)
- scripts/presubmit-orchestrator.sh (modified)
- tests/fixtures/presubmit/* (new)
- internal/ci/README.md (documentation for prober usage)

title: rollout-conductor-introduce-contract-lanes-and-playbooked-rollback
preview: Motivation: Multiple teams have been extending canary and shadow runtimes with ad-hoc safety checks and manual rollback runbooks, which leads to inconsistent behavior across Dedicated and Private deployments. This PR introduces a rollout conductor that formalizes 'contract lanes' - named SLO/contract driven lanes that encode metric gates, degradation policy, and automated playbooked rollback steps. The goal is to provide a single declarative place to define lifecycle behavior for canary/A-B/shadow experiments, allow per-customer overrides in Dedicated, and enable safe automated rollback with human-in-loop confirmation when required. What this changes: 1) a new CRD schema for RolloutContract and ContractLane (helm/templates/rollout-contract-crd.yaml), 2) a conductor service that evaluates lane gates against streaming metrics and traces and emits actions (services/rollout/conductor.go, runtimes/scheduler/slo_evaluator.py), 3) a compact domain-specific contract language for expressing latency, error, and quality gates (docs/contracts.md, services/rollout/contract_parser.go), 4) a set of curated playbook templates that the conductor can execute for automated rollback, canary hold, or graceful drain (ops/playbooks/rollback/*), 5) console UX to create/edit contract lanes and visualize active guard state (console/src/components/rollout/ContractLaneEditor.tsx), 6) tests and end-to-end harness that run a staged canary against a local metrics emulator. Backwards compatibility: existing simple canaries still function; the conductor is opt-in via a RolloutContract reference on the service annotation. Rollout plan: merge to main behind feature flag rollout.conductor.enabled, deploy conductor to staging, exercise with synthetic traffic, and then flip flag for a small cohort of customers before global rollout. Testing: unit tests for parser and evaluator, integration test harness simulating metric regressions, manual runbook rehearsals with SRE. text: Motivation: Multiple teams have been extending canary and shadow runtimes with ad-hoc safety checks and manual rollback runbooks, which leads to inconsistent behavior across Dedicated and Private deployments. This PR introduces a rollout conductor that formalizes 'contract lanes' - named SLO/contract driven lanes that encode metric gates, degradation policy, and automated playbooked rollback steps. The goal is to provide a single declarative place to define lifecycle behavior for canary/A-B/shadow experiments, allow per-customer overrides in Dedicated, and enable safe automated rollback with human-in-loop confirmation when required. title: multi-tool-execution: ergonomic API + parallel tool runner + result injection + retry policies
preview: Motivation: Improve ergonomics and reliability for tool/function-calling in the OpenAI-compat layer and native Redwood API. Customers asked for easy multi-tool invocations, deterministic injection of tool results into continuations, safe parallel tool execution, and more robust retry/backoff for transient tool failures. This PR introduces a small, backwards-compatible API extension plus a new parallel tool-runner implementation and runtime wiring for result injection and structured output hints. Summary of changes: 1) API: add `tools` invocation signature that supports multiple tool calls in a single step and an optional `parallel` flag. 2) Runtime: implement ParallelToolRunner with optimistic concurrency, coordinated token streaming, and ordered result injection points. 3) Retry semantics: add configurable retry/backoff policies per-tool with circuit-breaking thresholds and idempotency markers. 4) Structured output: expand function-calling schema to include `injection_point` and `merge_strategy` hints for deterministic consolidation of parallel results. 5) OpenAI compatibility: surface the new multi-tool signature through the OpenAI-compat endpoint with feature gating and compatibility warning headers. 6) SDK: Python and TS client updates for ergonomics and typed helpers. 7) Tests & docs: unit + integration tests for parallel tool execution, backpressure scenarios, and streaming stability; docs and migration guide. Implementation notes and tradeoffs: - Parallel execution is best-effort: by default the runner executes tools concurrently and injects results in the order of `injection_point` when present. If tools have cross-dependencies, callers should use explicit sequential mode or `depends_on` fields. - Streaming: we added a coordinator that merges streaming outputs from active tools into a single response stream while preserving token interleaving semantics. This required a small amount of additional buffering (configurable via runtime flags) to avoid head-of-line blocking. - Retries: only idempotent-marked tools will be automatically retried; non-idempotent tools can opt into safe-retry wrappers. Retries use exponential backoff with jitter and a per-tool max attempt limit. - Backwards compatibility: existing function-calling calls remain unchanged. The new fields are optional and gated behind an SDK flag and an OpenAI-compat header for the next release window. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed by runtime and API teams (ENG-8341) - [x] Prototype validated in perf-canary with small synthetic workload - [x] Unit tests for ParallelToolRunner and retry policies - [x] Integration tests for streaming merge and structured injection - [x] Python + TS SDK helpers + docs snippets - [x] Compatibility guard and migration notes in docs - [x] Load test showing <5% median latency regression on typical chat workloads Known limitations: - Result consolidation for very large tool outputs can increase memory usage; we recommend streaming-friendly tool outputs or chunking. - Deterministic ordering across parallel calls is only guaranteed when callers provide explicit injection points. Related: ENG-8341 (tool ergonomics), PM-412 (product requirements), SUP-2023 (support case that drove retries). See inline changed files for details.

title: elastic-wait-slope-scheduler-with-proactive-kv-hinting
preview: Context: In high-concurrency continuous-batching workloads we observed head-of-line effects where a small number of long KV-miss-heavy requests cause repeated batch hold-ups and elevated p99. Motivation: Provide a scheduler heuristic that favors low-wait-slope requests (likely short or cache-hit dominated) to reduce p99 tail while ensuring starvation-free progress for heavy requests. Design: Introduce an 'elastic wait-slope' metric computed per request as recent wait-time divided by estimated service tokens; the admission controller prefers requests with low wait-slope but maintains a dynamically-sized 'elastic window' that guarantees periodic admission of higher-slope requests. To prevent livelock we add a per-queue aging credit that increases admission priority linearly with time and is bounded by the window manager. KV cache integration: A lightweight prefetch hinter predicts likely KV cache misses from tokenized embeddings and request signatures and emits async prefetch requests to the KV layer; the scheduler consults a per-request kv_hint flag to bias admission and to pre-warm KV entries when possible. Timeouts & backoff: Implemented jittered soft-timeouts for queued requests and exponential backoff for repeated prefetch attempts to avoid thundering prefetch storms. Implementation notes: New modules: src/scheduler/wait_slope.rs (heuristic + admission), src/scheduler/window_manager.rs (elastic window logic), src/kv/prefetch_hint.rs (prediction + prefetch pipeline), small integration hooks in src/serve/integration.rs. Exposed runtime knobs in deploy/config/defaults.yaml and an ops page docs/ops/scheduler_tuning.md. Benchmarks: microbench harness (tests/perf/scheduler_bench.rs) shows 8-12% improvement in p99 latency under mixed KV-hit:miss workloads and ~4% average latency improvement; CPU usage increases by ~3-6% depending on workload due to prefetch overhead. Testing: unit and integration tests added; perf harness committed. Rollout: feature gated behind runtime flag scheduler.elastic_wait_slope and kv.prefetch.enabled; staged rollout via canary group then global. Risks: prefetch can increase load on KV; defaults are conservative. Migration: none required. Related: ENG-8124 implements metrics to detect prefetch overload; ENG-8201 ties this work to per-tenant QoS controls.

title: cyclic-attn: lane-stride rotary-shift fusion with loopback prefetcher
preview:
Motivation: reduce per-token latency for short-to-mid sequences and improve throughput for bursty, heterogeneous workloads by reducing memory traffic and improving kernel L2 reuse. This PR introduces a 'cyclic lane-stride' attention microkernel that fuses rotary/rope shift operations into the qk/v compute path and adds a small on-chip loopback prefetcher to warm the KV cache lanes during continuous batching.

Summary of changes: fused rotary-shift pass merged into the QK kernel to avoid standalone rotary transposes; introduced lane-stride tiling to improve SIMD coalescing on Ampere/Blackwell-class GPUs; added a lightweight loopback prefetcher that issues speculative KV loads for the next contiguous lane during continuous batching; extended the KV cache API with an "anticipated_lanes" hint used by the runtime scheduler for warmup; scheduler updates to support cyclic assignment of requests to lanes to minimize lane-switch penalties; microbench harness + unit tests and perf regression guardrails.

Checklist:
- [x] Microkernel implementation (CUDA + device intrinsics)
- [x] Runtime scheduler changes + continuous-batcher integration
- [x] KV cache extension and e2e tests
- [x] Benchmark harness and telemetry for new metrics
- [x] Documentation snippets in kernel/README and runtime/CHANGELOG

Benchmarks (internal harness, p99 reported as median across 5 runs):
- End-to-end latency (single-request, seq_len=128, batch=1): down 14% p99 compared to baseline attn-v2
- Throughput (mixed-length continuous batching, 256-token epoch): +18% tokens/sec
- GPU memory bandwidth observed: -9% host<->device transfers for typical chat workloads

Compatibility and fallbacks: When "anticipated_lanes" hint is not provided, runtime falls back to conservative lane allocation (no prefetch). On devices that do not support the lane-stride intrinsics we fall back to the previous QK kernel path (code is guarded with capability checks). The KV cache ownership model is unchanged; the hint is advisory only.

Related work: complements ENG-1831 (continuous batching improvements) and ENG-1824 (kv-warmup policies). This PR does not change model semantics.

Notes for reviewers: the CUDA microkernel is in kernel/cyclic_lane_qk.cu and contains device-level intrinsics; please pay attention to correctness under edge cases (odd-head counts, seq<tile). The scheduler changes are localized to runtime/scheduler/cyclic_allocator.{py,rs}.

title: Ensemble headroom calibrator and burst quieting for smoother autoscaling
preview:
Background and motivation:
Services using Dedicated fleets exhibit frequent autoscaler churning when tenant traffic arrives as short high-frequency microbursts. The existing single-signal headroom estimator (90th-percentile EWMA) under-reacts to transient demand and over-reacts when bursts happen repeatedly, producing oscillation. This PR implements two complementary mechanisms: 1) an ensemble headroom calibrator that combines multiple short- and medium-horizon predictors to produce a more stable headroom suggestion, and 2) a burst-quieting cooldown adapter that temporarily suppresses aggressive autoscaler decommits when microburst patterns are detected.

High-level summary of changes:
- runtime/autoscaler/ensemble_calibrator.go: New ensemble estimator that fuses EWMA, short AR(1) predictor, and a quantile sketch (P2 approximation) into a weighted headroom suggestion per-tenant. The weights adapt based on recent prediction error.
- runtime/autoscaler/cooldown_adapter.go: Burst quieting logic that creates a per-tenant quiet window after burst detection. During the window, scale-down decisions are delayed or softened (graceful decrease factor applied).
- runtime/telemetry/utilization_reporter.go: Added smoothed-percentile metrics (p50_smooth, p90_smooth, p99_smooth) and a burst_event counter/taggable by tenant and fleet. Telemetry uses the same batching path as existing metrics to avoid increased cardinality.
- config/defaults.yaml: New feature flags and tunables (ensemble.enabled, ensemble.weights, quieting.window_ms, quieting.threshold_tokens). Defaults are conservative (disabled by default) to allow staged rollout.
- test/autoscaler/ensemble_calibrator_test.go and test/e2e/burst_quieting_integration_test.go: Unit and integration tests covering estimator convergence and cooldown semantics under synthetic microburst traces.
- docs/observability/metrics.md: Documentation for new metrics and recommended dashboard panels.

Design notes and rationale:
- Why ensemble? No single estimator performed well across tenants with different burstiness profiles. The ensemble balances short-term sensitivity (AR(1)) and stability (EWMA + sketch quantiles). We adapt weights via a simple inverse-error scheme to avoid heavy meta-learning complexity.
- Why cooldown adapter? Autoscaler oscillation primarily occurred on scale-down paths when warm pool and decommit heuristics reacted to transient reductions. Quieting prevents premature contract of capacity that forces subsequent immediate scale-ups. The adapter is deliberately conservative: it only applies when burst detection confidence exceeds configured threshold and when warm pool utilization is below a safety cutoff.
- Telemetry trade-offs: To remain cost-conscious we reuse existing metric aggregation flows and avoid high-cardinality labels. Burst events are taggable by tenant id but emitted as sparse events (counter+last_seen timestamp) to support downstream anomaly detection without blowing ingestion costs.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge behind feature flag (ensemble.enabled=false).
2) Enable on a single non-critical dedicated fleet in canary mode with extra telemetry retention for two weeks.
3) If metrics show reduced scale churn and no SLO regressions, enable on 20% of fleets via targeting rules.
4) Full rollout to all dedicated fleets after 6 weeks of positive signals.
Feature flags allow immediate rollback.

Testing and validation:
- Unit tests cover estimator behavior across controlled traces (steady, ramp, repeated microburst).
- E2E replayed traces show 35–60% reduction in scale-down+scale-up cycles for microbursty tenants in canary fleet.
- Performance: ensemble_calibrator introduces a small CPU cost (~0.7% median p50 on autoscaler process); benchmarks attached.

Commits (summary):
- a1f3d4e: initial implementation of ensemble calibrator and interfaces
- b2d4c5f: add burst detection and cooldown adapter, wiring into autoscaler flow
- c3e5f6a: telemetry metrics and docs + feature flag integration
- d4f6a7b: unit & e2e tests, benchmarking harness, CI adjustments

Files changed (high-level):
- runtime/autoscaler/ensemble_calibrator.go
- runtime/autoscaler/cooldown_adapter.go
- runtime/telemetry/utilization_reporter.go
- config/defaults.yaml
- test/autoscaler/ensemble_calibrator_test.go
- test/e2e/burst_quieting_integration_test.go
- docs/observability/metrics.md

Review conversation (highlights):
- Lukas Meyer (review): "Can we guard the ensemble path with a strict CPU/budget check? Autoscaler loop is latency sensitive and we should gate extra cycles. Also please add an experiment flag for weights so we can A/B tune without code changes."
- Priya (author): "Added a cheap fast-path: if the tenant request rate is below min_samples we skip the ensemble and fall back to EWMA. We also exposed ensemble.weights via config and Linear ENG-2104 is tracking the tuning experiment."
- Aisha Patel (review): "Telemetry names look good. Please ensure burst_event emission doesn't add high-cardinality labels. Also we should emit a 'quieting_applied' gauge so SRE can monitor suppressed decommits."
- Priya: "Burst events remain sparse and tenant tags are optional; added quieting_applied as a boolean gauge per-tenant (low-cardinality since only applied tenants will report)."
- Diego Ramirez (approval): "Test coverage is good. I ran the e2e with production-like traces; behavior is stable. Approving subject to small nit on doc wording."

CI and checks:
- go vet: pass
- unit tests: pass (coverage +1.8%% for autoscaler pkg)
- integration tests: pass (e2e harness reproduced microburst scenario)
- lint: pass after minor fixes

Merge outcome:
- Merged via squash-merge to main after approvals. Feature gated behind ensemble.enabled=false by default.

Follow-ups / operational notes:
- ENG-2049 will track the weight-tuning experiment and dashboards for quieting effectiveness.
- Create a Grafana panel (suggested in docs) that correlates burst_event count with scale cycles per tenant for post-rollout evaluation.
- Monitor CPU impact on autoscaler process for the first 2 weeks of canary.

If you want a quick demo: checkout the branch and run test/e2e/replay.sh with testdata/microburst-tenant.json to see the quieting behavior and generated telemetry.

title: dynamic per-route payload limits + streaming proxy buffer tuning to stabilize function-calls and OpenAI-compatible chunking
preview:
Motivation: Several production routes (chat-stream, tools/function_call, and OpenAI-compat proxying) have experienced intermittent stalls and truncated function_call payloads when large intermediate tool outputs are streamed in small chunks. Root cause summary: a) statically-sized streaming proxy flush thresholds and buffer headroom do not match per-route payload patterns; b) global payload size limit was too coarse (caused safe-failures for tool outputs but allowed oversized non-streamed requests); c) OpenAI compatibility shim assumed final-message framing which breaks when intermediate buffer flushes split function_call JSON tokens across frames.

What this PR does (summary): Adds a dynamic per-route payload limiter and adaptive streaming-proxy buffer tuning. The gateway now derives two parameters per route: effective_payload_limit (bytes) and buffer_flush_threshold (bytes). Derivation uses route metadata (route type: chat|tooling|embedding), model family hints, and historical size percentiles (configurable window). To preserve OpenAI compatibility we introduce a small "reassemble-on-boundary" shim that guarantees function_call JSON objects are not emitted mid-object across chunk boundaries when possible (best-effort with a soft timeout).

Key changes (high level): - gateway/routes/policy: new PolicyWeights to compute effective_payload_limit per-route and expose policy endpoint for manual overrides. - gateway/proxy/stream_buffer: adaptive buffer with three modes: passthrough (low-latency), accumulate (when API route signals structured-output), and reassemble (OpenAI function_call safety window). - gateway/openai/compat: added streaming-function-call-shim that watches for "function_call" object starts and attempts to flush complete tokens when buffer thresholds are hit. - metrics and observability: new metrics gateway.route.payload_limit_bytes and gateway.proxy.reassemble_invocations with tagging by route/model. - docs: gateway/operational.md updated with rollout and a knob to pin per-route limits. - tests: new integration streaming test covering fragmented function_call frames and a perf benchmark for latency/cost tradeoffs.

Behavioral notes / compatibility: - Default behavior is conservative: effective_payload_limit defaults to previous global limit, only routes with historical large-percentile traffic will increase limits. title: regional-kv-pause-and-softbackoff-guard-hotfix
preview: Summary: Hotfix to reduce region-wide KV eviction storms that were causing transient SLO breaches during metadata-thrashing incidents. Root cause: under high regional egress the KV sticky-preserve heuristic allowed aggressive KV retention eviction loops that amplified load; eviction churn caused higher CPU on the serving runtime and led to regional retry storms. This PR introduces a defensive 'regional KV pause' gate and an exponential soft-backoff for sticky eviction decisions, plus extra telemetry and an emergency runbook link. Motivation and context: after incident SUP-9012 on 2026-02-18 we observed repeated reopen cycles tied to KV pressure; prior mitigations (short-term drain) prevented immediate outage but did not stop churn. The fix is intentionally conservative and primarily runtime-side so it can be backported to release branches. Changes in this PR: 1) runtime/eviction.go: introduce RegionKVPauseManager (lightweight gate) that rate-limits eviction churn for nodes within the same region and enforces a short circuit that prefers soft-eviction (drop non-hot prefixes) before full tenant eviction; 2) runtime/eviction_policy.go: add exponential soft-backoff state machine for sticky-preserve toggles (max backoff 30s with jitter); 3) controller/region_probe.go: expose a probe that surfaces kv_pressure_ratio and kv_eviction_churn metrics and a new API endpoint used by the control plane to annotate affected nodes; 4) telemetry/metrics.yaml: add kv_eviction_churn, kv_pause_events, kv_backoff_state metrics and Grafana dashboard fragment; 5) tests/regression_kv_shedding_test.go: deterministic regression that reproduces the pressure->churn loop and validates the pause/backoff suppresses oscillation; 6) docs/runbooks.md: emergency runbook entry "Regional KV pause and soft-backoff" and rollback instructions. Checklist: - [x] Reproduced churn in local benchmark harness - [x] Unit tests added and passing - [x] Integration scenario validated in benchlab (staging) - [x] Runbook written and reviewed - [x] Backport PRs prepared for release/2026-02-hotfix Notes on design tradeoffs: the RegionKVPauseManager is intentionally coarse-grained (region-level) to avoid per-tenant state bloat; we chose a soft-eviction-first approach to preserve hot KV prefixes whenever possible. This reduces tail-latency regressions at the cost of slightly longer memory residency for low-activity prefixes during the pause window. Follow-ups: - Consider adaptive backoff bounds driven by observed CPU/egress signals (ENG-4290) - Add per-tenant telemetry tagging for disputed prefixes (work item ENG-4354) Links: Incident SUP-9012 heatmap: https://redwood.internal/incidents/SUP-9012; Linear tasks: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4281.

title: cross-sdk-compat-layer-and-ergonomic-stream-consumer-batching
preview: Context: Multiple teams requested consistent streaming ergonomics and auth/reauth behavior across the three official SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go). This PR implements a lightweight compatibility layer in the monorepo to centralize retry profiles, stream consumer semantics, and signal/shutdown handling so SDKs can converge without duplicating policy logic. Motivation: parity gaps were causing subtle production regressions (different backoff curves, resume semantics, and platform shutdown behavior). Goals: 1) provide a single source of truth for retry/backoff presets used by all SDKs, 2) standardize streaming consumer APIs (async iterator in Python, async generator / AsyncIterable in TS, context-aware streamer in Go) and 3) add platform-specific shutdown handling (improved Windows Ctrl-C/signal handling) and a small set of batching presets for low-latency / high-throughput workloads. Summary of work: - New compat module: sdk/compat/retry_presets.go / retry_presets.py / retryPresets.ts which define named retry bundles (conservative, balanced, aggressive) and serialization hooks so SDKs can embed them at build time. - Streaming API harmonization: Python: introduced StreamClient.iter_lines() typed async iterator and StreamConsumer helper for backpressure hooks; TypeScript: added AsyncGenerator-based consumer helpers plus small adapter for Node/Browser differences; Go: added StreamCursor interface that accepts context.Context for clean cancellation and a StreamAdapter that mirrors TS/Py semantics. - Auth & reauth: centralized token refresh policy and heartbeat hint protocol so SDKs can exchange short lease tokens and run the same refresh jitter logic. - Batching presets: server + client hints to encourage smaller/larger batch windows depending on latency budget; clients surface an ergonomic batchingHint option. - Windows and signal handling: previously the Go SDK's interrupt logic was Linux-first; added a small cross-platform signal shim and e2e test to validate graceful KV cache flush on Ctrl-C on Windows runners. - Docs and examples: updated docs/sdk/streaming.md with cross-language examples and migration notes, added examples/streaming/prefix_cache.py and examples/ts/streaming_demo.ts. - Tests: unit tests in each SDK package for parity cases, integration smoke tests in ci/workflows/sdk-integration.yml. Checklist: - [x] compat/retry_presets implemented and exported - [x] Python async iterator facade added and documented - [x] TypeScript async generator helpers added and documented - [x] Go context-aware StreamCursor implemented - [x] Token refresh policy centralized and validated - [x] BatchingHint option surfaced in client constructors - [x] Windows signal shim + e2e test added - [x] CI matrix updated to run py/ts/go streaming suites - [x] Version bumps for all three SDKs Local testing: ran unit tests across SDKs and the integration smoke suite locally and on CI windows-latest, ubuntu-latest, macos-latest. Observability: added short-lived telemetry hooks so the platform can measure retry counts and reauth latencies (opt-in by default to protect privacy). Rollout plan: merged to main behind feature gate; SDKs will publish subsequent releases once the compatibility package lands in the build artifacts. Breaking changes: none by default; new ergonomic APIs are additive and migration notes are in the docs. Related: references ENG-2345 (streaming parity), ENG-4512 (token lease refactor), SUP-987 (customer-reported reconnects).

title: Introduce 'Thrifty' inference profile generator for conservative batching/caching/quant presets
preview: Adds a new service+CLI to synthesize conservative, production-safe default inference profiles from short trace samples. Motivation: many customers hesitate to enable aggressive batching/quantization/caching because they lack templates that balance unit-economics wins with latency/regression risk. This change introduces 'thrifty' profiles that err on the side of lower cost while preserving tail-latency SLOs. Key pieces: 1) trace-sampler: lightweight offline analyzer that ingests representative request traces and outputs distribution summaries (seq len, token mix, prefix reuse). 2) profile-generator: rules engine that maps trace summaries to a prioritized preset (batch size, prefetch window, prefix cache policy, int8/4 quantization candidate with safety fences). 3) rw-profile-gen CLI and Docker image for customers to run locally or in infra pipelines. 4) integration with recommendations API so Console can surface the generated profile and a one-click apply to dedicated/private deployments. The generator targets conservative defaults (microbatching on high-latency tails, small prefix caches for low-reuse workloads, disabled quant for short sequences) and emits an explainable rationale for each knob. Includes unit tests, e2e smoketest in the perf-canary harness, and docs for operators. This PR intentionally avoids aggressive heuristics (no automatic live rollouts) — it emits profiles as suggestions and supports a manual canary apply flow. Related: ENG-10233 (profile UX), SUP-3281 (customer support case triggered by runaway token costs).

title: Introduce influence-matrix eval hook and rollback scorer
preview:
Motivation: We've had recurring incidents where a small prompt tweak in a PR produced outsized regressions for a narrow but critical slice of prompt-sets. Existing CI smokechecks flag macro regressions but provide little signal about which prompts, tokens, or candidate edits are most influential. This PR adds an 'influence matrix' eval hook that attributes per-prompt and per-perturbation influence on aggregate metrics, and a lightweight rollback scorer that suggests the smallest prompt change(s) that would return metrics to baseline.

What this does: Implements an eval hook that runs alongside existing regression checks. For each prompt-set it computes an influence score per prompt and per change vector using a randomized leave-k-out approximation (bounded sampling plus importance-weighting) and aggregates into an influence matrix (prompt x perturbation). The CI adapter consumes aggregated scores and will soft-block PRs that exceed a configurable impact threshold, surface a ranked list of top-k harmful prompts in the PR UI, and attach a rollback_scorer report with candidate minimal edits (text span revert or constraint relaxations) with estimated delta impact.

Design notes: The algorithm favors speed and interpretability over exact Shapley values; we approximate contribution via stratified sampling and an anchored baseline. Influence values are normalized per-metric and can be weighted by metric severity (e.g., hallucination > BLEU-like). The rollback scorer uses greedy local search over candidate revert spans and prompt parameter toggles (system message, temperature hints) to propose edits with confidence bands.

Backward compatibility: Hook is opt-in per-eval-job via job config and defaults to disabled for long-running production backfills. When enabled it adds a bounded extra eval cost (~10-30% depending on cohort size). The hook exposes tunables: sample_budget, top_k_reports, soft_block_threshold, metric_weights.

Files changed: See files_changed field for a concise list.

Related: ENG-2319 (detect high-impact prompt regressions ahead of rollout), PM-412 (UI work to display ranked prompt influence in PR).

title: Add invocation cohorting and kernel-warmup heatmap with adaptive alert backoff
preview: Motivation: We see repeated noisy alerts driven by small cohorts of invocations (odd prompt shapes or very long KV cache cold starts). Teams need a way to attribute cost/latency anomalies to invocation cohorts and to visualize how kernel latency evolves across the first N tokens as kernels and KV caches warm up. This PR introduces: 1) a lightweight per-invocation cohorting tag (invocation_cohort_id) based on prompt-shape and context-warmth heuristics; 2) a kernel-warmup heatmap that aggregates kernel-level latency for the first 32 tokens vs KV-warmth buckets and exposes a Grafana dashboard; 3) an adaptive alert-backoff rule that quiets alerts for cohorts that show high variance but low long-term impact, and increases sampling and digesting instead of immediate paging. Checklist: - [x] Add cohort id generation and propagation into traces and metrics - [x] Emit cohort-level rollup counters and histograms (latency, tokens, cost-est) - [x] Instrument kernel-level per-token timings and KV-warmth tag - [x] Grafana dashboard JSON and panels for heatmap + cohort explorer - [x] Alert rule: adaptive backoff with cohort decay window and prioritization - [x] Unit tests and integration test for cohort assignment - [x] Docs: observability guide section + example query snippets Design summary: - Cohorting: we compute a compact prompt-shape fingerprint = hash(num_tokens, avg_token_length, normalized_prompt_embedding_bucket) and combine with a KV-warmth score (0..3) to form invocation_cohort_id = fingerprint:vwarm. This is intentionally coarse to bound cardinality while grouping meaningful shapes. Cohort id is attached as a trace tag and a metric label for rollups. - Kernel warmup heatmap: the collector records per-kernel micro-latency for token positions 0..31 and emits a histogram keyed by (kernel_name, token_pos_bucket, kv_warmth). We downsample high-frequency kernels with reservoir sampling for storage cost. - Adaptive alert backoff: new alert logic uses cohort-level variance + recent impact to decide whether an alert should escalate to paging or be converted into a digest entry. This reduces churn for cohorts with spiky behavior but small aggregate cost. Commits (high-level): - commit 1: "observability: add invocation cohort id and cohort propagation" - introduces cohort fingerprinting code, tagging in request tracer, and metric label plumbing. - commit 2: "collector: kernel warmup timing and kv-warmth tag" - records per-token kernel latencies, adds reservations for high-cardinality limits, and backfills unit tests. - commit 3: "monitoring: grafana kernel-warmup heatmap + cohort explorer" - adds dashboard JSON + example queries. - commit 4: "alerting: adaptive cohort backoff rule and playbook" - updates alert rules and runbook guidance. Files changed (high level): - src/observability/cohorting.rs: new fingerprinting and tag propagation logic - src/collector/kernel_timer.cc: per-token kernel timers and kv-warmth extraction - src/collector/rollups.rs: cohort rollup counters and histograms - deploy/monitoring/grafana/dashboards/kernel_warmup_heatmap.json: new dashboard (heatmap + cohort explorer panels) - infra/alerts/alert_rules.yaml: adaptive backoff rule and new labels - sdk/python/redwood/observability.py: helper to surface cohort id in SDK traces - docs/observability/cohorting.md: design/queries and runbook - tests/observability/test_cohorting.py: unit tests for fingerprint boundaries - examples/observability/query_examples.md: example PromQL / Flux queries Migration notes: - Cohort id is an additive label; existing metrics are unchanged but rollups gain a new label. We add default cardinality caps to collectors (default 5k cohorts per shard) to avoid explosion; teams with high cardinality can opt-in for higher caps via config. Observability queries (examples included in docs): - Top cohorts by token-cost: sum(token_cost) by (invocation_cohort_id) - Kernel warmup heatmap: heatmap of p95 kernel_latency per token_pos_bucket grouped by kv_warmth - Cohort stability metric: delta of p95 over 5h-window and 1h-window Review summary: - Diego found an edge-case where trivial prompt shapes collided; we widened the hash input to include a small fingerprint of the first non-empty token. - Priya suggested lowering reservoir sample size for the hottest kernels; adjusted to 10k -> 5k to cap storage. - Liam requested an explicit opt-out flag for compliance-sensitive customers; added config flag and test. Backwards compatibility: additive only. Telemetry and cost: added a small instrumentation overhead (~0.7% sampling equivalent) when heatmap enabled; default is off for low-traffic tiers. Merge: Squashed and merged to main on 2026-02-24.

Files changed (high level): - src/observability/cohorting.rs: new fingerprinting and tag propagation logic - src/collector/kernel_timer.cc: per-token kernel timers and kv-warmth extraction - src/collector/rollups.rs: cohort rollup counters and histograms - deploy/monitoring/grafana/dashboards/kernel_warmup_heatmap.json: new dashboard (heatmap + cohort explorer panels) - infra/alerts/alert_rules.yaml: adaptive backoff rule and new labels - sdk/python/redwood/observability.py: helper to surface cohort id in SDK traces - docs/observability/cohorting.md: design/queries and runbook - tests/observability/test_cohorting.py: unit tests for fingerprint boundaries - examples/observability/query_examples.md: example PromQL / Flux queries Migration notes: - Cohort id is an additive label; existing metrics are unchanged but rollups gain a new label. We add default cardinality caps to collectors (default 5k cohorts per shard) to avoid explosion; teams with high cardinality can opt-in for higher caps via config. Backwards compatibility: additive only. Telemetry and cost: added a small instrumentation overhead (~0.7% sampling equivalent) when heatmap enabled; default is off for low-traffic tiers. Merge: Squashed and merged to main on 2026-02-24.

title: accrual-ledger-for-tenant-bursting-and-thresholded-refill
preview:
Context and motivation:
This PR introduces an 'accrual ledger' for Dedicated fleets: a lightweight per-tenant credit accounting system that lets tenants accumulate unused capacity as credits and spend them for controlled bursts later. Motivation came from multiple customers whose bursty workloads either starved other tenants or caused over-provisioning. Instead of hard reservation or coarse burst tokens, the accrual approach provides fine-grained economics-aware bursting with safety fences.

Design highlights:
- AccrualKeeper component (control-plane): keeps an EWMA-based balance per tenant and enforces a max-credit cap. Credits accrue when tenant utilization is below a configurable target window (default: 5m EWMA w/ 30th percentile headroom).
- Thresholded refill: when a tenant's credit drops below refill_threshold%, the system will gradually grant refill tokens from a dedicated warm pool subject to cluster headroom limits and fairness constraints.
- Spend policy: tenants consume credits at request admission time; consumption is proportional to requested concurrency and seqlen estimate (tokens). Excessive spend is rate-limited by a soft per-minute drain.
- Fairness & safety: cross-tenant fairness implemented via proportional-share weights and a short-term anti-saturation clamp to prevent single-tenant hogging during refill windows.
- Telemetry: new metrics (tenant_credit_balance, tenant_credit_accrual_rate, tenant_refill_granted, tenant_credit_spend) + paging-friendly alerts for declining cluster-wide accrual health.

Why different from prior work:
- Unlike burst token pools that assign a fixed token budget, accrual credits are a continuous accounting primitive tied to actual underutilization, making sustained burst patterns predictable and reducing wasted reserved capacity.
- Works with existing warm-pool and headroom controllers via a thin admission proxy; does not replace autoscaler decisions but provides a tenant-visible budget signal used for admission/fallback.

Checklist:
- [x] Implement control-plane AccrualKeeper and API surface (gRPC)
- [x] Integrate admission-side spend checks in dedicated admission-proxy
- [x] Metrics + opencensus wiring and Grafana dashboard stub
- [x] Unit + integration tests for accrual math and refill gating
- [x] Canary e2e against small dedicated pool in CI
- [x] Feature gate: enabled by default for dedicated clusters but controllable via cluster config

Operational knobs (config keys):
- accrual.window_ms (default 300000)
- accrual.max_credit_per_tenant (default 10000 units)
- accrual.refill_threshold_pct (default 25)
- accrual.refill_rate_limit_per_min (default 2000 units/min)
- accrual.weight_overrides (per-tenant map)

Compatibility and rollout plan:
- Backwards-compatible: if AccrualKeeper is disabled or not present, admission-proxy falls back to existing burst token logic.
- Rollout: feature-flagged. Start with canary on one Dedicated pool, monitor tenant_credit_balance and tenant_refill_granted.
- Rollback: clear the feature flag & drain in-progress refill grants; metrics and logs allow reconstruction of state for auditing.

Testing notes:
- Unit tests cover accrual math under synthetic workloads and ensure no negative balances; added 26 unit tests.
- Integration test adds a synthetic workload in perf-canary that toggles between 20% and 180% of baseline to verify accrual/refill behavior.
- Added property tests for monotonic accrual under constant underutilization and bounded drains under continuous high spend.

Open items and followups (post-merge):
- Analytics: map tenant credit usage to per-token cost for billing (ENG-3499).
- Autoscaler integration: allow autoscaler to read aggregated credits for smarter scaling (future PR).

References:
- ENG-3456: tenant burst fairness initiative
- internal doc: RFC - accrual-ledger (linked in Linear ticket)

title: Introduce auditor-proxy RBAC role and KMS failover shim with region-pin rewrite
preview:
Motivation: Several enterprise customers and our compliance team requested a hardened, auditable read-only access path for internal auditors and third-party compliance reviewers that does not increase the blast radius nor allow data export. This PR introduces an auditor-proxy RBAC role and a KMS failover shim that enforces region-pin rewrite semantics for audit reads so that decryption is always bound to the data residency constraints.

Scope: Single PR implementing RBAC v2 policy artifacts, an audit-forwarder shim that mediates auditor requests, a KMS failover path that supports per-request region pin rewriting, console UI for granting the auditor-proxy role, and Terraform bindings to create the minimal IAM resources. The feature is feature-flagged behind enable_auditor_proxy.

What changed (high level):
- New RBAC policy: auditor_proxy_v1 (read-only, no-export guardrails, session-level attestations).
- Audit-forwarder service: authenticated proxy that strips all write-capable tokens and issues ephemeral read-only audit tokens scoped to a KEK (ephemeral key-encryption-key).
- KMS shim: failover path that attempts the preferred regional KMS, then transparently rewrites region pin on the audit token when permitted by residency policy and falls back to a customer-approved cross-region key with explicit exception logging.
- Console: small role-grant modal and audit-log discovery view updates with new filter for proxy sessions.
- Terraform: new iam bindings and optional audit-proxy module for Dedicated/Private installs.

Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-4382) [x] unit tests for policy compiler and shim [x] integration test harness for the audit-forwarder [x] docs (security/auditor-proxy.md) [x] console feature flag + e2e smoke test (canary) [x] manual rollout plan added to PR description

Rollout plan: feature-flag default disabled. Stage: canary in internal staging cluster -> limited enterprise beta (opt-in) -> default opt-in for new Dedicated installs. We will run telemetry for 2 weeks in canary and monitor key metrics: (1) audit-forwarder latency P50/P99, (2) KMS extra-hop rates, (3) number of proxy sessions, (4) audit-log-only errors. A rollback is a feature-flag flip.

Security considerations: the auditor-proxy role is intentionally constrained:
- No direct access to plaintext model inputs/outputs via API (audit-forwarder returns redacted records by default).
- Session-scoped ephemeral KEK delegation only for decryption of audit envelopes where customer policy permits.
- All cross-region key fallbacks require a signed exception in the audit envelope and are rate-limited and logged for SOC review.

Compatibility/migration: existing RBAC v1 consumers unaffected. Customers with custom RBAC v2 policies should review policy merging notes in docs. Terraform module includes an opt-in migration script that converts an allowlist entry into the auditor-proxy binding.

Notes: This PR intentionally avoids introducing new long-lived service principals; audit access uses ephemeral tokens minted by the audit-forwarder with short TTL and attestation hooks for operator review.

title: weighted-queue-and-kv-pin-timeouts-to-mitigate-starvation
preview: Context: Production traces and perf canaries exposed recurring tail-latency regressions under mixed workloads with hot and cold tenants. Root cause analysis showed that the continuous batching scheduler's FIFO-dominant queue allowed small high-priority requests to be repeatedly overtaken when the runtime favored large batched prefixes; simultaneously the KV cache had a long pin expiry that amplified hot-key monopolization. This PR introduces two coordinated changes: 1) a lightweight weighted-queue throttler in the continuous batcher that enforces service-share weights at queue admission time (soft scheduling), and 2) adjustable KV-cache pin timeouts and a small-metadata eviction hint to reduce long-running pin monopolies. Design: - Admissions now record a per-queue weight token and a sliding-window deficit counter; long-running peers lose admission weight over configurable windows to prevent starvation. - The scheduler still batches by model/seq-length compatibility, but before combining candidates we apply a fast O(1) fairness filter that reorders a small prefix of the queue based on deficit and age. This preserves batching efficiency for the common case while bounding unfairness. - KV cache: introduce per-tenant pin_ttl (default lowered from 30s -> 8s) and a 'soft-evict' hint that allows cold-key compaction during sustained high memory pressure. - Feature gate: both features are behind runtime flags (batcher.weighted_queue=true, kv.pin_adaptive=true) and can be tuned via config map in Dedicated/Private. Implementation notes: - runtime/scheduler/continuous_batcher.rs: added WeightedQueue struct, deficit accounting, admission helpers, unit tests for corner cases. - runtime/scheduler/queue.rs: added peek-and-reorder primitive with micro-optimizations to avoid allocations. - runtime/kv_cache/pinning.rs: introduced pin_ttl, soft_evict hints, metrics for pin_count and pin_ttl_hist. - tests/scheduler_integration.rs: new deterministic multi-tenant load harness and long-tail assertion checks. - docs/ops/continuous-batcher-tuning.md: operator-facing notes and suggested knob values for common workloads. Performance summary: - Canary synthetic workload (10 tenants; mix heavy/light): P95 request latency reduced by 22% for light tenants and P99 reduced by 14% overall when feature gate enabled; end-to-end throughput unchanged within 3%. - Microbench: weighted admission adds ~150ns median overhead per admission check; observed <1% CPU cost at 100k qps. Testing and rollout: - Unit tests added for deficit accounting, corner cases for zero-weight tenants, and KV pin expiry behaviour. - Integration canary run in staging for 48h before merge. - Rollout plan: enable behind feature gate for 10% of Dedicated clusters, observe tail metrics for 72h, then ramp to 100% with monitor playbook. Backwards compatibility: defaults preserve existing behavior (feature gates off). Related work: builds on ENG-7120 (continuous-batcher refactor) and pairs with upcoming kernel-level scheduling hints (ENG-8350).

title: Introduce presubmit fast-cache + dev-dep visibility adapter
preview:
Motivation: CI presubmit lanes have regressed in median feedback time due to cold CI images and noisy dev-dependency churn. This PR introduces a presubmit "fast-cache" layer for our CI runner image and a small dependency-visibility adapter that enforces an explicit dev-dep manifest to prevent accidental transitive dev-dependency installs.

Goals: 1) reduce end-to-end presubmit feedback time for lint/test jobs by warming a lightweight artifact cache (node_modules tarballs, pip wheels) scoped to the PR diff; 2) make dev-dependency changes explicit so CI cache invalidation is more intentional and less noisy; 3) avoid large monolithic CI image rebuilds on each dev-dep bump.

What this changes: - new CI helper: .github/presubmit/cache-warmup.sh (shell helper run in presubmit lanes to warm cache from fast-cache service) - presubmit fast-cache service client: infra/ci/fastcache/ (minimal go client + config) - new lightweight CI image Dockerfile: infra/ci/docker/presubmit-fast.Dockerfile (smaller base + preinstalled runtime libs) - dev-dep visibility manifest: dev-deps.yml at repo root and per-language dev-deps.{json|toml} used by the presubmit adapter - workflow changes: .github/workflows/presubmit.yml updated to call warmup early and fall back to existing caching when unavailable - package management: package.json scripts + pip tool updates to support manifest lock queries

Non-goals: this PR does not change production runtime images, dedicated capacity autoscaling, or release pipelines.

Implementation notes: - Fast-cache is opt-in per-repo and per-branch; we added config flags in the global ci-config to gate rollout. - The dev-dep adapter validates that any changed dev-deps are reflected in dev-deps.yml; absent an entry, the job will fail presubmit with a guidance message and a one-click suggestion to run ./scripts/devdep-sync.sh. - The cache warmup step uses a transitive-diff key that only includes files in the PR diff that affect dependency resolution (package.json, requirements.txt, poetry.lock, go.mod, etc.) to avoid unnecessary cache invalidations.

Testing and rollout: - Iterative rollout behind a feature flag: default off for main, on for feature/* branches and canary teams. - Unit + e2e tests added for the fast-cache client (infra/ci/fastcache/tests) and a flaky-presubmit integration test that simulates cold-run vs warmed-run and asserts median latency drop. - Manual canary: rolled out to the infra-team CI first for two weeks before enabling repo-level gate.

Checklist: - [x] add fastcache client and config - [x] add cache warmup runner and integrate into presubmit workflow - [x] add dev-deps manifest + adapter + sync script - [x] update README and developer docs with onboarding steps - [x] add unit and integration tests - [x] add feature-flag gate and rollback plan

Performance: in local canary runs, warmed presubmit reduced average lint/unit feedback from ~19m to ~7m (median) for a typical web-service PR with ~120 changed files.

Risk and rollback: if fastcache has transient failures we fallback to existing restore-from-cache logic and proceed; dev-dep manifest failures are safe-fail and provide clear remediation steps.

Related: ENG-4312 (presubmit perf), ENG-4476 (ci-image fragmentation), PM-62 (developer velocity metrics).
