title: introduce-metric-dependency-graph-and-alert-orchestrator
preview: Motivation: We frequently see noisy correlated signals where a downstream alert creates a cascade of symptom alerts and noisy paging. This PR introduces a lightweight metric dependency graph and an alert orchestrator that uses that graph to: 1) compute transitive impact scores for alerts, 2) suppress or de-duplicate follow-on alerts for 60s by default, and 3) publish derived metrics (root-cause-likelihood, alert-impact-score) for dashboards and SLO decomposition. Design summary: - graph_builder: constructs a directed weighted graph from time-series cross-correlation and historical co-incident alert windows. We persist the graph as a compact adjacency list and per-edge confidence scores. - orchestrator: a service that subscribes to alert events and consults the graph to decide suppression, priority escalation, and annotated alert payloads. - derived metrics: we emit root_cause_prob and impact_score per-alert as time series with tags (route, model, tenant). - dashboard: new "Metric Dependency Explorer" dashboard that visualizes the graph, highlights high-impact nodes, and provides a ranked list of candidate root causes for a selected incident. - token-level tie-in: when an alert is related to inference performance we annotate impact_score with token_cost_delta and tail_latency_fraction to assist SREs in triage. Rollout plan: - deploy graph_builder and orchestrator to staging behind feature flag (obs.dep_graph=true). - collect 72h of graph telemetry, validate edges against known incidents. - enable passive mode (annotate-only) for 24h in prod, then enable suppression for a small subset of tenants. - full rollout after two production incidents validated and no regressions in alerting noise. Migration notes: - existing alert pipelines are unchanged when feature flag is off. - a small database migration adds dep_graph_v1 table; migration script included and is backwards compatible. Checklists: - [x] Graph construction pipeline implemented and unit tested. - [x] Orchestrator service with circuit breaker and rate limit implemented. - [x] Dashboard panels and quickstart doc added. - [x] Integration tests in qa/staging for end-to-end alert flow. - [x] Runbook draft linked. Testing: - unit tests cover graph heuristics and suppression logic. - synthetic incident generator used to validate no increased false negatives on a sample of historical incidents. - load test: orchestrator sustained 10k alerts/min with 12ms median decision latency in staging. Risks & mitigations: - risk: incorrect graph edges could suppress a true root alert. mitigation: default to annotate-only mode for new edges below confidence threshold 0.6; suppression requires confidence >= 0.85. - risk: increased cardinality on derived metrics. mitigation: we bucket tenant tags for derived metrics and sample low-volume tenants. Notes for reviewers: focus on graph weighting heuristics (graph_builder/weights.py) and suppression safety checks (orchestrator/safety.go). Link to runbook: https://linear.app/redwood/obs/dep-graph-runbook. Related: ENG-1429 (alert noise reduction), ENG-1484 (token-cost triage integration).

This PR introduces a lightweight metric dependency graph and an alert orchestrator that uses that graph to: 1) compute transitive impact scores for alerts, 2) suppress or de-duplicate follow-on alerts for 60s by default, and 3) publish derived metrics (root-cause-likelihood, alert-impact-score) for dashboards and SLO decomposition. We persist the graph as a compact adjacency list and per-edge confidence scores. - orchestrator: a service that subscribes to alert events and consults the graph to decide suppression, priority escalation, and annotated alert payloads. - derived metrics: we emit root_cause_prob and impact_score per-alert as time series with tags (route, model, tenant). - dashboard: new "Metric Dependency Explorer" dashboard that visualizes the graph, highlights high-impact nodes, and provides a ranked list of candidate root causes for a selected incident. - token-level tie-in: when an alert is related to inference performance we annotate impact_score with token_cost_delta and tail_latency_fraction to assist SREs in triage. Rollout plan: - deploy graph_builder and orchestrator to staging behind feature flag (obs.dep_graph=true). - collect 72h of graph telemetry, validate edges against known incidents. - enable passive mode (annotate-only) for 24h in prod, then enable suppression for a small subset of tenants. - full rollout after two production incidents validated and no regressions in alerting noise. Migration notes: - existing alert pipelines are unchanged when feature flag is off. - a small database migration adds dep_graph_v1 table; migration script included and is backwards compatible. Checklists: - [x] Graph construction pipeline implemented and unit tested. - [x] Orchestrator service with circuit breaker and rate limit implemented. - [x] Dashboard panels and quickstart doc added. - [x] Integration tests in qa/staging for end-to-end alert flow. - [x] Runbook draft linked. Testing: - unit tests cover graph heuristics and suppression logic. - synthetic incident generator used to validate no increased false negatives on a sample of historical incidents. - load test: orchestrator sustained 10k alerts/min with 12ms median decision latency in staging. Risks & mitigations: - risk: incorrect graph edges could suppress a true root alert. mitigation: default to annotate-only mode for new edges below confidence threshold 0.6; suppression requires confidence >= 0.85. - risk: increased cardinality on derived metrics. mitigation: we bucket tenant tags for derived metrics and sample low-volume tenants. Notes for reviewers: focus on graph weighting heuristics (graph_builder/weights.py) and suppression safety checks (orchestrator/safety.go). Link to runbook: https://linear.app/redwood/obs/dep-graph-runbook. Related: ENG-1429 (alert noise reduction), ENG-1484 (token-cost triage integration).

title: oncall handoff checksum and telemetry synthesizer for compact context transfer
preview: Motivation: handoffs during high-pressure incidents frequently lost critical context (partial traces, clipped logs, ad-hoc snippets). This PR adds two complementary features to reduce costly context gaps: 1) a deterministic handoff checksum attached to every incident-bot handoff message that verifies payload fidelity across tools; 2) a telemetry synthesizer service that constructs a compact, privacy-safe summary of missing spans/events when downstream telemetry is sparse. The checksum is a canonical JSON serialization + SHA256 digest with stable ordering and configurable salt, surfaced as 'handoff_checksum' in handoff payloads and displayed in the oncall UI; operators can use it to assert the handoff message received by downstream runbooks/Slack threads matches the sent content. The telemetry synthesizer runs as an opt-in stage during handoff: it inspects available traces/logs, infers any missing 'critical markers' (eg. deploy markers, leader-loss events, DB failovers) using lightweight heuristics, and emits a synthetic summary under a retention TTL and privacy filters (PII redaction). Implementation notes: added new CLI flags --handoff-checksum and --synth-telemetry-ttl; backend uses an in-memory LRU cache to dedupe synth outputs; synth output includes provenance metadata and confidence scores for each synthesized item. Checklist: - implement checksum generation and attach to handoff payloads - add synth heuristics and TTL-based retention - UI: show checksum and synth summary link in handoff modal - tests: unit tests for canonicalization, synth heuristics, and e2e handoff flow - docs: upgrade oncall runbook template with checksum verification steps High level commits: 1) add checksum generator and unit tests 2) add telemetry synthesizer module + heuristics 3) wire CLI flags and config parsing 4) UI changes to surface checksum and synth links 5) tests, docs, and changelog. Changed areas: backend/handshake/handshake.go (checksum logic), backend/synth/synthesizer.py (heuristics engine), cli/handoff_cli.go (flags), webapp/src/components/HandoffModal.tsx (UI), docs/oncall/handoff-checksum.md, tests/{unit,e2e}_handoff_test.*. Review conversation summary: Liam O'Connor: 'Strong utility, but ensure TTL and privacy filters are explicit and configurable.' Maya: 'Added TTL flag and explicit redaction step; default TTL set to 7 days and can be shortened per-org.' Priya Natarajan: 'Can we gate synth by incident severity? Least-privilege for low-priority incidents.' Maya: 'Added severity gating (configurable threshold) and included examples in docs.' Diego Alvarez: 'Please add load tests for the synth path.' Maya: 'Added simple load smoke test and circuit-breaker to prevent synth storms.' CI summary: initial run failed lint on webapp; fixed formatting and re-ran CI; final run passed all checks (unit, lint, e2e smoke). Merge outcome: merged via squash-merge, changelog entry added to release notes. Release notes: 'improve oncall handoffs: include deterministic checksum for handoff payloads and optional synthesized telemetry summaries to fill sparse traces; configurable TTL and severity gating; docs updated with verification steps and examples.'

title: Session-pinned canary with deterministic shadow replay and graceful drain
preview: Motivation: reduce noisy user-visible regressions during canary promotions by ensuring session-affine traffic stays pinned while enabling deterministic shadow replay for offline verification before promotion. This PR implements a session-pinned canary lane that pins user sessions to the incumbent variant until a session-safe handoff completes and introduces a deterministic shadow replay path that replays sampled live requests into candidate models with preserved session state. Key goals: maintain session consistency for stateful flows, provide reproducible shadow results for automated regression detectors, and support graceful drain with end-to-end idempotency guarantees. Design summary: 1) Session affinity tokens: requests now carry a stable session token used by the canary conductor to decide pinning eligibility. 2) Handoff protocol: a three-phase handshake (prepare, verify, commit) that verifies KV-cache compatibility and replay parity before committing session transfers. 3) Deterministic shadow replay: captured request + session KV is replayed through the candidate in a deterministic executor that disables non-deterministic hooks and seeds RNGs. Replays are recorded to the shadow-results store for batch evaluation. 4) Graceful drain and rewind: if a safety gate trips, affected sessions are rewound to incumbent within the same flow using snapshot checkpointing. 5) Metrics and telemetry: new histograms for replay-latency, replay-diff-score, and session-handoff-duration; integrated into console rollout dashboard. Checklist: - [x] core conductor protocol and handshake implementation - [x] deterministic executor and replay capture - [x] session snapshotting and rewind hooks - [x] telemetry, tracing spans, and dashboard wiring - [x] unit and e2e smoke tests - [x] rollout playbook and runbook entry Backwards compatibility: falls back to existing stateless canaries when session tokens are absent. Migration notes: to enable session-pinned canaries an operator flag must be set and a short config migration on Dedicated clusters performed to enable checkpoint storage. Security: session tokens are treated as opaque identifiers and not logged; replay store encrypted with existing KMS keys. Commit summary: small focused commits for protocol and tests followed by a squash for release. See commits below. CI: flaky integration test (replay-suite) retried once; all checks passed on final run. Merge strategy: squash and merge. Impact: primarily rollout conductor and runtime executor changes; rollout safety gates tightened. Release notes: Adds session-aware canaries and deterministic shadow replay for safer model promotions, enabled via operator flag. No breaking API changes.

title: Circuit stabilizer: connection quieting and tenant SLO-tilt hotpatch
preview: Motivation: During an incident on 2026-02-14 we observed rapid circuit reopen/reclose oscillations for certain islanded regions which amplified load and caused tenant-level SLO violations. Root cause analysis showed two compounding issues: (1) the circuit reconciler could race when reconciling restored health probes and reopen attempts, and (2) the shedder decision logic treated all SLO deficits equally, causing aggressive shedding for tenants where small latency increases were acceptable and for others where impact was severe. This PR introduces a targeted hotpatch that (a) adds a short 'connection quieting' window after a circuit state change to prevent immediate reopen retries, (b) introduces a tenant SLO-tilt score that biases the regional shedder away from high-impact tenants when feasible, and (c) fixes the reconcilation race by serializing state transitions through a single reconcile queue per-region. The change is intentionally conservative: default quiet window is 3s and tilt weight defaults to 0.2 to avoid large policy swings. Changes: - core/slo/tilt.go: new tilt scoring util and config hooks that expose a per-tenant weight used by the shedder. - serving/circuit/reconciler.go: serialized region reconcile queue plus idempotent transition checks to prevent flip-flopping. - serving/circuit/quieting.go: small in-memory LRU that tracks recent transitions and prevents reopen attempts until quiet window expires. - serving/shedder/region_decision.go: incorporate tilt score into weighted shedding decision; maintain preferred fallback model selection when high-tilt tenants are present. - telemetry/events.go: emit QuietWindowStart/QuietWindowEnd and TiltScoreComputed events. - integration/e2e/quieting_test.go: deterministic test that simulates probe flapping and asserts no oscillatory reopen. Backwards compatibility and rollout: defaults preserve current behavior for customers who do not tune tilt or quieting via runtime config. Feature is behind a runtime flag `enable_connection_quieting` (true in this hotfix rollout). We will canary the change to the US-west region for 24h before global rollout. Observability: added two new histograms (quiet_window_duration, tilt_score) and two counters for quieting hits/misses. Telemetry tags include tenant_id and region. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for tilt scoring and reconcile queue - [x] Integration e2e that reproduces the incident pattern - [x] Telemetry emits validated in CI - [x] Manual canary in us-west for 24h - [x] Documentation note in CHANGELOG and runtime flags section Risks and mitigations: The quiet window could increase recovery latency for true healthy nodes; mitigated by keeping default low (3s) and exposing config for operators. Tilt weighting could under-shed a tenant that contributes to global load — the weight is advisory and the shedder still enforces capacity caps. Rollback plan: revert runtime flag or roll back the deployment; reconciler changes are backward-compatible because they are serialized but do not change transition semantics. Contact: SRE on-call + Priya for triage.

title: Commitment signal multiplexer and safe throttle proxy
preview: Motivation: We see recurring incidents where autoscaler decisions diverge from external commitment inputs (billing/reservation overrides, SRE manual patches, and predictive headroom hints), causing transient overcommit or overly-conservative throttles. This PR introduces a Commitment Signal Multiplexer and a Safe Throttle Proxy to 1) consolidate multiple commitment sources into a single normalized commitment feed, 2) apply conservative safety rules and smoothing for downstream autoscalers and per-tenant throttle enforcement, and 3) provide observability for reconciliation reasoning. High-level changes: - pkg/autoscaler/commitment_mux: new multiplexer that ingests: reservation ledger commits, predictive headroom hints, manual overrides (Linear/console), and observed utilization signal. It publishes a time-series normalized_commitment{tenant} with provenance tags and confidence scores. Multiplexer implements configurable priority ordering, hysteresis windows, and a small Bayesian smoothing step to avoid flips on noisy signals. - pkg/throttle/proxy: safe-throttle-proxy sits in front of the per-tenant throttle gate. It consumes normalized_commitment and will (a) clamp outgoing throttle budgets if observed instantaneous QPS would violate commitment+headroom, (b) emit a soft-fallback token path that falls back to lower-cost model variants when capacity tight, and (c) surface a recommended autoscaler_adjustment suggestion via the autoscaler API. - Telemetry: new metrics (redwood_commitment_normalized, redwood_commitment_adjustments_total, redwood_throttle_clamps, redwood_throttle_suggested_adjustments) and an events stream for mismatch reasons. - Config/ops: feature gate enable_commitment_mux (off by default), per-tenant policy overrides with allowlist/denylist, and a rollout plan with canary-only for reserved pools. - Backward compatibility: when the feature is off, autoscaler reads remain unchanged. Multiplexer mode supports a passthrough profile to mirror existing behavior for rollout. - Testing: unit tests for mux priority/hysteresis, integration e2e that replays reservation ledger events and predictive hints, and smoke test for the safe-throttle proxy with synthetic bursts. - Migration: small DB migration to add provenance columns to the reservation ledger (safely backfilled), and a short-lived compatibility shim for older controllers. The description below enumerates the diff surface, CI outcome, and review thread. Overall risk: medium; runtime changes touch the hot path for throttle decisions but are guarded behind a feature gate and incremental canary rollout. Rollout plan: enable in canary reserved pool for 48h -> expand to 10% of dedicated capacity -> full rollout with monitoring checks for throttle clamp rates and autoscaler churn.

title: lease-based-idempotency-and-adaptive-retry-window-in-openai-adapter
preview:
Context and motivation:
We keep seeing customer production failures where naive client retries (especially across streaming tool-invocations) either trigger duplicate side-effects or fail to respect per-customer transient capacity limits. Previous effort focused on clarifying 429 semantics and idempotency key formats, but two pain points remain: (1) idempotency keys are long-lived and encourage reuse across logically distinct operations, and (2) the server's "Retry-After" hints are either too sparse or ambiguous for streaming/tool-call flows.

This PR implements two coordinated server-side changes in the OpenAI compatibility layer and the toolkit used by tool-calling/runbook integrations: (A) lease-based idempotency tokens (short-lived, single-use leases that carry scope and validation metadata), and (B) an adaptive retry-window envelope returned in a structured response header/frame to guide client backoff with finer granularity and jitter hints. Together these reduce duplicate side-effects while giving clients better signals to avoid hot retries during capacity pressure.

What this changes (high level):
- New header: X-Idempotency-Lease: a short opaque lease token issued on request acceptance when idempotency is requested via the existing Idempotency-Key. Leases are single-use and time-bound (default TTL 60s, configurable per route).
- New structured retry envelope header: Retry-Hint: base64url(JSON) where JSON = {"retry_after_ms": int, "jitter_ms": int, "lease_backoff": "boolean", "scope": "string", "hint_version": "v1"}. The header is attached to non-success responses (429/503/504) and to a final control frame for streamed failures.
- Streaming contract: for streamed responses we add a final control SSE frame (event:control, data: <base64url JSON error envelope>) when an in-stream error occurs; the envelope includes the same fields as Retry-Hint plus an error_code and ephemeral_lease (if applicable).
- Idempotency refinement: the adapter now issues ephemeral leases when the incoming Idempotency-Key is detected but we need a bounded scope to protect internal retries. Clients may present either an Idempotency-Key (legacy) or the X-Idempotency-Lease (preferred after initial acceptance). Leases are validated atomically with the operation and revoked on completion.
- Error normalization: unify error_code strings to the format namespace.action.error (e.g., "rate_limit.write.exceeded", "tool.execution.timeout"). We also return a top-level canonical message and an errors[] list for richer clients.

Design notes and rationale:
- Lease model avoids reuse across disconnected operations: a long-lived API token (Idempotency-Key) is still honored to derive intent, but any actual deduplication uses the short-lived lease which guarantees bounded state and simpler revocation semantics for retries/failover.
- Retry-Hint carries a jitter_ms value so SDKs can implement randomized backoff within server-specified bounds; lease_backoff=true indicates clients should renew/obtain a fresh lease before retrying tool-invocations that have side-effects.
- Streaming control frame ensures that streamed tool-call failures are surfaced with the same structured guidance as regular responses and that intermediate frames are not misinterpreted as retriable success.

API compatibility and client guidance:
- Backwards compatible: existing clients that only look at HTTP 429/Retry-After will continue to work. New clients can prefer Retry-Hint/X-Idempotency-Lease. If both Idempotency-Key and X-Idempotency-Lease are supplied, lease wins for deduplication semantics.
- SDK changes (separate PRs ongoing for python/ts): SDKs should automatically parse Retry-Hint, honor jitter_ms, and only retry tool-calls when lease_backoff=false OR after acquiring a fresh lease. Streaming clients should listen for control frames and follow the envelope rather than heuristics like connection close times.

Implementation summary (files changed):
- src/openai_adapter/handler.go: add header parsing and lease issuance/validation, attach Retry-Hint on error paths.
- src/openai_adapter/streaming_control.go: new helper to emit final control frames for streaming failures.
- internal/idempotency/lease_store.go: implement in-memory + redis-backed lease store with TTL and revocation.
- internal/errors/normalize.go: map internal error categories to canonical error_code strings.
- tests/integration/openai_adapter_retry_test.go: add tests for lease lifecycle, header parsing, streaming control frames.
- docs/openai_adapter.md: document header formats, example sequences, and migration guidance.
- sdk-adapters/compat/README.md: short note for SDK maintainers.

Commits (summary):
- 7e2f1b2: introduce lease store interface and redis implementation
- 9a4d3c8: wire lease issuance into request acceptance path in adapter handler
- a63f9e1: emit Retry-Hint header for 429/503 and populate structured envelope for streaming failures
- b2c4d69: normalize error codes and update tests for canonical messages
- c4d5e8a: docs + migration guidance + small refactor of existing idempotency unit tests

Testing and CI:
- Added unit tests for lease TTL/renewal/reject semantics.
- Integration tests cover: (1) client retries using Idempotency-Key results in unused lease; (2) client uses lease and server rejects second use; (3) Retry-Hint header contains jitter_ms and sensible bounds; (4) streaming flow includes final control frame when a tool-call errors mid-stream.
- CI initially failed on a flaky timing test in integration (intermittent sleep races). I adjusted the test harness to use deterministic fake clock for lease TTLs and re-ran CI. Final run: all checks green (lint / unit / integration / e2e).

Security and operational considerations:
- Leases are opaque tokens and rotate regularly; we intentionally keep leases single-use and short TTL to reduce blast radius if leaked.
- Redis-backed lease store offers eventual consistency trade-offs; we added an atomic compare-and-revoke step to avoid double-accepts on concurrent requests. For weaker backends we fall back to a best-effort strategy and emit metrics when collisions are observed.
- Monitoring: new metrics lease.issued, lease.used, lease.rejected, retry_hint.emitted. Added dashboard to SLO team for early detection.

Review conversation (high level):
- Diego Ramos: "Nice approach. Two asks: add a test for concurrent lease race on our redis cluster emulator and ensure the lease size is limited (no enormous headers)."
- Maya (author): "Added concurrent race test using the emulator and capped lease token size to 128 bytes, storing longer entropy server-side."
- Priya Natarajan: "Can we avoid clients having to parse base64 headers? Would an alt header be better?"
- Maya: "We considered JSON header but some proxies strip complex headers; base64url(JSON) keeps a single header safe-cross-proxy. SDKs will expose helpers to decode. Documented in docs/openai_adapter.md."
- Samir Goyal: "Streaming control frame payload: confirm we don't expose secrets in envelopes and that they are signed if we later allow cross-service retries."
- Maya: "Control frames contain only non-sensitive fields and ephemeral_lease tokens. We validated with security — if we later enable cross-service lease handoff we'll add HMAC signatures and audit logs."

Merge outcome:
- Merge method: squash and merge.
- Post-merge actions: SDK teams were pinged (PR references in python/ts SDK repos) with migration checklist and example code for parsing Retry-Hint and using X-Idempotency-Lease. Ops scheduled a 24h canary rollout for high-throughput routes to monitor lease collision rates.

Risk and next steps:
- Risk: client adoption lag may lead to mixed behavior during rollout; mitigated by backwards compatibility and conservative default TTLs.
- Next steps: follow-up PRs to add HMAC-signed leases for inter-service handoff, SDK updates to expose helpers, and optional telemetry headers for per-request sampling of retry decisions.

If you'd like, I can split the lease store implementation into a standalone package and open a follow-up to add signed lease tokens.
text:
Context and motivation:
We keep seeing customer production failures where naive client retries (especially across streaming tool-invocations) either trigger duplicate side-effects or fail to respect per-customer transient capacity limits. Previous effort focused on clarifying 429 semantics and idempotency key formats, but two pain points remain: (1) idempotency keys are long-lived and encourage reuse across logically distinct operations, and (2) the server's "Retry-After" hints are either too sparse or ambiguous for streaming/tool-call flows.

This PR implements two coordinated server-side changes in the OpenAI compatibility layer and the toolkit used by tool-calling/runbook integrations: (A) lease-based idempotency tokens (short-lived, single-use leases that carry scope and validation metadata), and (B) an adaptive retry-window envelope returned in a structured response header/frame to guide client backoff with finer granularity and jitter hints. Together these reduce duplicate side-effects while giving clients better signals to avoid hot retries during capacity pressure.

What this changes (high level):
- New header: X-Idempotency-Lease: a short opaque lease token issued on request acceptance when idempotency is requested via the existing Idempotency-Key. Leases are single-use and time-bound (default TTL 60s, configurable per route).
- New structured retry envelope header: Retry-Hint: base64url(JSON) where JSON = {"retry_after_ms": int, "jitter_ms": int, "lease_backoff": "boolean", "scope": "string", "hint_version": "v1"}. The header is attached to non-success responses (429/503/504) and to a final control frame for streamed failures.
- Streaming contract: for streamed responses we add a final control SSE frame (event:control, data: <base64url JSON error envelope>) when an in-stream error occurs; the envelope includes the same fields as Retry-Hint plus an error_code and ephemeral_lease (if applicable).
- Idempotency refinement: the adapter now issues ephemeral leases when the incoming Idempotency-Key is detected but we need a bounded scope to protect internal retries. Clients may present either an Idempotency-Key (legacy) or the X-Idempotency-Lease (preferred after initial acceptance). Leases are validated atomically with the operation and revoked on completion.
- Error normalization: unify error_code strings to the format namespace.action.error (e.g., "rate_limit.write.exceeded", "tool.execution.timeout"). We also return a top-level canonical message and an errors[] list for richer clients.

Design notes and rationale:
- Lease model avoids reuse across disconnected operations: a long-lived API token (Idempotency-Key) is still honored to derive intent, but any actual deduplication uses the short-lived lease which guarantees bounded state and simpler revocation semantics for retries/failover.
- Retry-Hint carries a jitter_ms value so SDKs can implement randomized backoff within server-specified bounds; lease_backoff=true indicates clients should renew/obtain a fresh lease before retrying tool-invocations that have side-effects.
- Streaming control frame ensures that streamed tool-call failures are surfaced with the same structured guidance as regular responses and that intermediate frames are not misinterpreted as retriable success.

API compatibility and client guidance:
- Backwards compatible: existing clients that only look at HTTP 429/Retry-After will continue to work. New clients can prefer Retry-Hint/X-Idempotency-Lease. If both Idempotency-Key and X-Idempotency-Lease are supplied, lease wins for deduplication semantics.
- SDK changes (separate PRs ongoing for python/ts): SDKs should automatically parse Retry-Hint, honor jitter_ms, and only retry tool-calls when lease_backoff=false OR after acquiring a fresh lease. Streaming clients should listen for control frames and follow the envelope rather than heuristics like connection close times.

Implementation summary (files changed):
- src/openai_adapter/handler.go: add header parsing and lease issuance/validation, attach Retry-Hint on error paths.
- src/openai_adapter/streaming_control.go: new helper to emit final control frames for streaming failures.
- internal/idempotency/lease_store.go: implement in-memory + redis-backed lease store with TTL and revocation.
- internal/errors/normalize.go: map internal error categories to canonical error_code strings.
- tests/integration/openai_adapter_retry_test.go: add tests for lease lifecycle, header parsing, streaming control frames.
- docs/openai_adapter.md: document header formats, example sequences, and migration guidance.
- sdk-adapters/compat/README.md: short note for SDK maintainers.

Commits (summary):
- 7e2f1b2: introduce lease store interface and redis implementation
- 9a4d3c8: wire lease issuance into request acceptance path in adapter handler
- a63f9e1: emit Retry-Hint header for 429/503 and populate structured envelope for streaming failures
- b2c4d69: normalize error codes and update tests for canonical messages
- c4d5e8a: docs + migration guidance + small refactor of existing idempotency unit tests

Testing and CI:
- Added unit tests for lease TTL/renewal/reject semantics.
- Integration tests cover: (1) client retries using Idempotency-Key results in unused lease; (2) client uses lease and server rejects second use; (3) Retry-Hint header contains jitter_ms and sensible bounds; (4) streaming flow includes final control frame when a tool-call errors mid-stream.
- CI initially failed on a flaky timing test in integration (intermittent sleep races). I adjusted the test harness to use deterministic fake clock for lease TTLs and re-ran CI. Final run: all checks green (lint / unit / integration / e2e).

Security and operational considerations:
- Leases are opaque tokens and rotate regularly; we intentionally keep leases single-use and short TTL to reduce blast radius if leaked.
- Redis-backed lease store offers eventual consistency trade-offs; we added an atomic compare-and-revoke step to avoid double-accepts on concurrent requests. For weaker backends we fall back to a best-effort strategy and emit metrics when collisions are observed.
- Monitoring: new metrics lease.issued, lease.used, lease.rejected, retry_hint.emitted. Added dashboard to SLO team for early detection.

Review conversation (high level):
- Diego Ramos: "Nice approach. Two asks: add a test for concurrent lease race on our redis cluster emulator and ensure the lease size is limited (no enormous headers)."
- Maya (author): "Added concurrent race test using the emulator and capped lease token size to 128 bytes, storing longer entropy server-side."
- Priya Natarajan: "Can we avoid clients having to parse base64 headers? Would an alt header be better?"
- Maya: "We considered JSON header but some proxies strip complex headers; base64url(JSON) keeps a single header safe-cross-proxy. SDKs will expose helpers to decode. Documented in docs/openai_adapter.md."
- Samir Goyal: "Streaming control frame payload: confirm we don't expose secrets in envelopes and that they are signed if we later allow cross-service retries."
- Maya: "Control frames contain only non-sensitive fields and ephemeral_lease tokens. We validated with security — if we later enable cross-service lease handoff we'll add HMAC signatures and audit logs."

Merge outcome:
- Merge method: squash and merge.
- Post-merge actions: SDK teams were pinged (PR references in python/ts SDK repos) with migration checklist and example code for parsing Retry-Hint and using X-Idempotency-Lease. Ops scheduled a 24h canary rollout for high-throughput routes to monitor lease collision rates.

Risk and next steps:
- Risk: client adoption lag may lead to mixed behavior during rollout; mitigated by backwards compatibility and conservative default TTLs.
- Next steps: follow-up PRs to add HMAC-signed leases for inter-service handoff, SDK updates to expose helpers, and optional telemetry headers for per-request sampling of retry decisions.

If you'd like, I can split the lease store implementation into a standalone package and open a follow-up to add signed lease tokens.

title: introduce-ecohistorian-batch-indexer-and-defaults-generator
preview:
Motivation: We repeatedly see suboptimal tenant defaults for batching and cache sizing because current heuristics don't leverage historical batch shapes and cross-tenant cache hit signals. This PR adds an offline "ecohistorian" indexer service that ingests anonymized serving telemetry and produces tenant-specific candidate defaults (batch shape templates, KV cache size suggestions, and conservative quantization hints). The goal is automation: feed the optimizer with statistically grounded defaults instead of hand-tuned heuristics.

What this PR does (summary): 1) New service pkg/ecohistorian: a lightweight indexer that summarizes sequence-length distributions, inter-request spacing, prefix reuse, and cache hit rates into compact signatures. 2) Generator module in tools/econ-defaults: consumes historian signatures to output tenant defaults as YAML/JSON (batching.default, cache.size_hint, quant.profile_candidates). 3) New CLI: `redwood econ-historian import --source <s3/snapshot>` to run the indexer on historical telemetry snapshots and produce a preview diff against current defaults. 4) API: a new GET /internal/econ/defaults-preview endpoint (auth gated) to preview suggested defaults for a tenant. 5) Telemetry: added metrics (ecohistorian.jobs, econ.defaults_suggested, econ.preview_delta_tokens_saved_estimate) and a small dashboard JSON for Console. 6) Tests and CI: unit tests for the indexing pipeline and an integration smoke test that runs the generator on a synthetic snapshot.

Design notes and safety: suggestions are conservative by default. Quant profiles are proposed with an explicit safety score; the generator never emits an aggressive quantization candidate without a fallback path. Suggested defaults include controlled fallbacks: when a suggested quantization is chosen, the system will route a configurable percentage of traffic to a non-quantized variant for 24 hours to detect regressions. The preview endpoint returns both the candidate and the safety metadata.

Checklist:
- [x] historian indexer implementation (pkg/ecohistorian)
- [x] defaults generator CLI and integration (tools/econ-defaults)
- [x] internal preview API and auth gating
- [x] metrics and Console dashboard fragment
- [x] unit + integration tests
- [x] docs: short howto under docs/ecosystem/econ-historian.md

Commits (high level):
- initial historian schema and ingestion pipeline
- summarizer: sequence length & inter-arrival histograms
- cache-hit and prefix-reuse aggregators
- defaults generator and YAML exporter
- internal preview API + routing and auth checks
- CLI + docs + tests

Changed files (high-level):
- pkg/ecohistorian/indexer.go (new)
- pkg/ecohistorian/summary.go (new)
- tools/econ-defaults/generator.go (new)

title: Add stochastic concurrency fence and staggered probe scheduler to reduce probe-induced noise
preview:
Context and motivation: We observed a recurring class of false-positive canary regressions when probe traffic arrives in short bursts or when client-side concurrency spikes align with canary probe windows. These cases amplify tail latency and create high variance in the canary signal, causing unnecessary rollbacks and developer churn. This PR implements two complementary mitigations: a stochastic concurrency fence (probabilistic admission control) to smooth bursty concurrent requests and a staggered probe scheduler to desynchronize probe timing across hosts.

What this changes (high level):
- Add perf-canary detector that computes `concurrency_entropy` per host and enables a probabilistic cap when entropy exceeds threshold.
- Replace existing probe dispatcher with a staggered scheduler that shards probe time windows to hosts and backfills missed probes without amplifying traffic.
- Export new prometheus metrics and tracing spans for debugging (see docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md).

Checklist: [x] design doc (perf-canary/docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md) [x] unit tests for fence and scheduler [x] integration stress test (tests/integ/canary_stress.sh) [x] backward-compatible config defaults [x] feature-flagged rollout in console

Implementation notes: The stochastic fence uses a lightweight token counter per runtime worker plus a small RNG-based drop decision to probabilistically reject new speculative requests once the measured concurrency distribution entropy rises above a configured baseline. The fence is parameterized by window_ms, entropy_threshold, and drop_p_max; drop probability grows with measured excess entropy to avoid hard cuts. The staggered probe scheduler assigns a deterministic offset per host based on host-id hashing and a jitter seed; it cooperates with the fence so probes don't get entirely rejected.

Safety and rollout: The feature is behind `perf.canary.stochastic_fence` flag and defaults to disabled in release builds. The canary pipeline now evaluates `perf_canary.concurrency_entropy` during pre-rollout shoulders and will auto-abort rollouts if entropy increases > 2x baseline and tail-p95 latency deviates > 20ms. We added a soft-fallback that disables the fence automatically if overall throughput drops > 5% relative to baseline.

Testing and benchmarks: Microbenchmarks show a 40% drop in variance for p99 under synthetic burst traffic and no measurable throughput regression (<1% difference) with default params. Integration stress runs ran for 2 hours across multiple hosts; one flaky e2e test failed initially due to an off-by-one in the offset calculation (fixed in commit 3) and CI was rerun.

Related: ENG-8421 (noise-reduction effort), ENG-8502 (canary signal stability). Design doc: perf-canary/docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md.

Please focus reviews on: correctness of entropy calc, safety of the fallback heuristics, and Prometheus naming/labels.

title: elastic-deadline-smoothing-with-occupancy-crediting-and-kv-prefetch-hysteresis
preview:
Motivation:
High-variance arrival patterns combined with KV warmups produce occasional tail spikes in the continuous batcher even when average utilization is healthy. Our SLOs are sensitive to these tails for interactive endpoints. This change introduces an \"elastic deadline smoothing\" layer in the scheduler that smooths slot admission deadlines across short horizons and a complementary \"occupancy crediting\" mechanism that temporarily increases admission priority for lanes that have been starved. In addition, the KV prefetcher was augmented with a hysteresis window to avoid synchronized prefetch storms during cold-starts.

What this PR does (summary):
- Adds serving/scheduler/elastic_deadline_smoother.rs: a scheduler stage that maintains a short rolling deadline histogram and applies a smoothing kernel (exponential decay) to outgoing admission deadlines to avoid sharp deadline oscillations.
- Adds serving/scheduler/occupancy_credit.rs: credits lanes that have been waiting beyond a configurable threshold; credits decay when lane receives service. Credits are additive with existing priority and respect per-customer caps.
- Adds serving/kv/prefetcher.rs changes: introduces a hysteresis interval (configurable) and randomized backoff seeds to desync prefetch attempts.
- Exposes new metrics: scheduler.elastic_deadline_smoothed_p95, scheduler.occupancy_credit_balance, kv.prefetch_hysteresis_skips.
- Adds integration tests and microbenchmarks that simulate bursty arrivals and KV cold-warm transitions.

Design notes and rationale:
- Smoothing vs hard deadlines: previous approaches used immediate deadline bumps that created ripple effects; smoothing prevents oscillatory re-prioritization by low-latency lanes.
- Occupancy crediting intentionally favors starved lanes for a limited time to avoid long tail starvation for low-QPS customers. Credits have an upper bound to prevent unfair bypass of SLA tiers.
- KV prefetch hysteresis prevents negative emergent behavior when many small requests would all trigger prefetch simultaneously after a cache eviction. Randomized jitter reduces correlated attempts across workers.

Checklist:
- [x] Design doc link: https://linear.redwood.ai/ENG-3421
- [x] Unit tests added for smoother and credit math
- [x] Integration test for continuous batching fairness (tests/integration/continuous_batching_timeout_test.go)
- [x] Benchmarks included (benchmarks/bench_deadline_smoothing.py)
- [x] Metrics and dashboards stubbed out in console/metrics/occupancy_exporter.go
- [x] Backwards-compatible config defaults (feature disabled by default for some tenants)

Performance impact (observed):
- End-to-end benchmark with mixed small/medium sequences, 10k QPS synthetic: p95 reduced by ~6.2%, p99 reduced by ~9.8% across a 5-minute tail window.
- KV-prefetch hysteresis reduced simultaneous prefetch attempts by ~72% in cold-start scenarios, decreasing cache backend load and secondary latency spikes.
- Average token latency unchanged (+0.5ms noise), overall throughput maintained.

Compatibility and rollout strategy:
- Feature gated via config: scheduler.elastic_deadline.enabled (default: false for Dedicated/Private until next release)
- Rolling canary rollout path included: canary rate ramp + metrics alert on credit-balance skew and p99 regressions.
- Console notes: added a short doc and dashboard for tracking occupancy credits and smoothing impact.

Risks and mitigations:
- Misconfigured credit caps could starve high-tier lanes: validated via unit tests and runtime assertions; default caps conservative.
- Smoothing kernel could delay urgent requests in pathological inputs: we bound smoothing window and expose max per-request latency jitter config.

Related work and references:
- Related PRs: ENG-27103 (continuous batching tail latency mitigations), PRs around kv-warmup and prefetch heuristics.
- Internal doc: docs/perf/elastic_deadline.md

Notes on testing/CI behavior:
- First CI run failed due to a flake in tests/scheduler/test_hooks (race on global clock mock). Fixed by serializing use of mock clock in tests and added a guard in tests. Subsequent run passed all checks.

Merge outcome:
- Merged via squash-merge after approval from the runtime owners and a green CI run.

Contact: @maya (Slack) for follow-ups or rollback if anomalies observed.
